id: AAFP 2021 - Using AI Assistant Reduce Documentation Burden Family Medicine
title: Using an AI Assistant to Reduce Documentation Burden in Family Medicine
authors: American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP) Innovation Lab
doi: None — grey literature (AAFP Innovation Lab report)
journal: American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP) Innovation Lab Report | 2021
product: **Suki AI** — AI assistant for documentation in family medicine. Labeled "AI Assistant for Documentation" in this report; Suki confirmed from external sources (Chief Healthcare Executive, Suki's own reposting of report).
setting: Family medicine / primary care — AAFP Innovation Lab Phase 2. 132 family physicians and primary care clinicians across the US; 3-practice initial Phase 1 pilot followed by national Phase 2. Voluntary trial period of 30 days.
evidenceLevel: Grey Literature (professional association innovation lab report; not peer-reviewed)
dataSources: Survey/self-report (n=40 qualitative survey); product telemetry / quantitative adoption data (n=132 documentation time); operational metrics (adoption/purchasing)
summary: AAFP Innovation Lab Phase 2 report evaluating Suki AI for documentation among 132 family medicine and primary care physicians over a 30-day trial. Of 132 participants, 102 completed the trial; 61 (60%) adopted Suki as paying customers post-trial. Adopters saw a 72% reduction in median documentation time per note, translating to ~3.3 hours/week of time savings. Phase 1 (10 physicians, 3 practices) found 62% reduction in in-clinic documentation time and 76% reduction in after-hours documentation time.
hypothesis: Not explicitly stated. Aim: assess whether AI-assisted documentation (Suki) is essential to and readily adopted by family physicians; evaluate adoption, impact, and barriers.

measures.description: % of trial participants who purchased Suki after trial; 60% (61/102)
% reduction in median per-note documentation time among adopters; 72%
Calculated hours saved per clinician per week; 3.3 hours
Phase 1 finding: 62% reduction in documentation time during clinic hours
Phase 1 finding: 76% reduction in after-hours documentation
Self-reported satisfaction with workload and practice

keyFindings: 60% adoption rate — 61 of 102 trial completers purchased Suki after trial
72% reduction in median documentation time per note among adopters
3.3 hours/week time savings per adopting clinician
Participants reported improved satisfaction with workload and overall practice
Non-adopters fell into 4 categories: (1) no significant documentation burden at baseline, (2) EMR workflow already efficient, (3) EMR integration not available, (4) couldn't fully complete the trial
Phase 1 (10 physicians, 3 practices):
62% reduction in in-clinic documentation time
76% reduction in after-hours documentation time
Described as "a breakthrough" by some clinicians

id: Afshar 2025 - Novel Playbook for Pragmatic Trial Operations
title: A Novel Playbook for Pragmatic Trial Operations to Monitor and Evaluate Ambient Artificial Intelligence in Clinical Practice
authors: Afshar, Majid; Resnik, Felice; Baumann, Mary Ryan; Hintzke, Josie; Lemmon, Kayla; Sullivan, Anne Gravel; Shah, Tina; Stordalen, Anthony; Oberst, Michael; Dambach, Jason; Mrotek, Leigh Ann; Quinn, Mariah; Abramson, Kirsten; Kleinschmidt, Peter; Brazelton, Tom; Twedt, Heidi; Kunstman, David; Wills, Graham; Long, John; Patterson, Brian W.; Liao, Frank J.; Rasmussen, Stacy; Burnside, Elizabeth; Goswami, Cherodeep; Gordon, Joel E.
journal: NEJM AI | Vol. 2, Issue 9 | Published: August 28, 2025
product: [[Abridge]] (©Abridge AI, Inc.), integrated with [[Epic]] EHR via FHIR R4 APIs and Epic Haiku mobile application at University of Wisconsin Hospitals and Clinics.
setting: Multi-specialty outpatient care at University of Wisconsin Hospitals and Clinics (UW Health). 66 providers enrolled across 8 specialties. Providers required to treat ≥20 patients/week and use Epic Haiku.
evidenceLevel: Quality Improvement (implementation phase) + RCT protocol (trial design described; primary results pending)
dataSources: Product telemetry | EHR data | Survey/self-report | Mixed
summary: This paper presents the "PTOps Playbook" — a pragmatic trial operations framework for implementing and evaluating ambient AI in routine clinical care. The team ran an implementation phase at UW Health using Abridge integrated with Epic, establishing governance, technical workflows, and real-time monitoring dashboards. Key findings included a 65.4% weighted median utilization rate and a temporary drop in ICD-10 coding accuracy (79% → 35%) due to a note template change, which recovered after redesign and retraining. The paper also describes the design for a 24-week stepped-wedge RCT powered for provider well-being as the primary outcome.
hypothesis: Integrating ambient AI into clinical workflows using a bidirectional governance model and a real-time monitoring framework will yield high adoption within a defined operational timeline and achieve acceptable documentation accuracy and workflow efficiency.

measures.description: Proportion of eligible clinical notes actually completed using the ambient AI system. Measured as weighted median across providers over time; captures real-world adoption behavior.
Time providers spend actively writing or editing notes within the EHR. Tracked via Epic metadata; assesses whether ambient AI reduces direct documentation effort.
Time spent on EHR documentation outside scheduled work hours. A proxy for after-hours administrative burden and indicator of potential burnout risk reduction.
measures.dimensions: II - Individual Impact, US - User Satisfaction

measures.description: Accuracy of diagnostic codes in AI-generated notes, audited using an internally developed LLM validated against certified professional coders (Pearson's r=0.97).
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, OI - Organizational Impact

measures.description: Primary RCT outcome; assessed via validated survey instruments. Designed to detect meaningful change in burnout and professional satisfaction across 66 providers.
Rate at which providers obtained and documented required patient consent before ambient AI recording. Monitored in real time; ensures regulatory and ethical compliance.
Time from end of patient encounter to note completion and signature. Tracks whether ambient AI accelerates the documentation cycle relative to standard practice.
Qualitative and survey-based assessments of provider satisfaction, usability concerns, and workflow friction. Collected via REDCap; drives iterative PDSA improvement cycles.
measures.dimensions: US - User Satisfaction, SerQ - Service Quality

id: Afshar 2025b - Pragmatic RCT Ambient AI Health Practitioner Wellbeing
title: A Pragmatic Randomized Controlled Trial of Ambient Artificial Intelligence to Improve Health Practitioner Well-Being
authors: Afshar, Majid; Ryan Baumann, Mary; Resnik, Felice; Hintzke, Josie; Gravel Sullivan, Anne; Wills, Graham; Lemmon, Kayla; Dambach, Jason; Mrotek, Leigh Ann; Quinn, Mariah; Abramson, Kirsten; Kleinschmidt, Peter; Brazelton, Thomas B.; Leaf, Margaret A.; Twedt, Heidi; Kunstman, David; Patterson, Brian; Liao, Frank; Rasmussen, Stacy; Burnside, Elizabeth S.; Goswami, Cherodeep; Gordon, Joel
journal: NEJM AI | Vol. 2, No. 12 | Published: November 26, 2025 | Received: August 20, 2025; Accepted: September 28, 2025
product: **Abridge AI, Inc.** — HIPAA-compliant ambient AI documentation platform integrated with Epic via private APIs and FHIR R4 APIs. EHR-embedded, documentation-only (no diagnostic/therapeutic decision support). Deployed version: generally available release as of October 10, 2024.
setting: Ambulatory clinics — UW Health (University of Wisconsin) across Wisconsin and Illinois. Eight specialties. Academic health system. Trial period: August 21, 2024–March 27, 2025. 66 health care practitioners (attending physicians + advanced practice providers).
evidenceLevel: RCT (stepped-wedge, individually randomized pragmatic trial — 24 weeks)
dataSources: - EHR audit-log data (time on notes, WoW, same-day closure)
summary: The most methodologically rigorous trial of ambient AI documentation in the corpus to date. A 24-week stepped-wedge individually randomized pragmatic RCT at UW Health (Wisconsin + Illinois), enrolling 66 health care practitioners across 8 specialties using Abridge AI integrated with Epic. 71,487 total notes authored; 27,092 (38%) generated using ambient AI. Coprimary outcomes: Stanford PFI work exhaustion/interpersonal disengagement (−0.44 points; p<0.001, **significant**) and professional fulfillment (+0.14 points; p=0.04, **nonsignificant by pre-specified alpha of 0.025**). Secondary: documentation time −0.36 hours/day (significant); WoW −0.50 hours/day (significant but sensitive to extreme outliers); billing code compliance improved (p<0.001); PDSQI-9 scores 3.97–4.99/5.0 (high quality); no software performance drift detected.
hypothesis: Ambient AI will improve health care practitioners' well-being, yielding higher fulfillment scores and lower work exhaustion/interpersonal disengagement (burnout) scores.

measures.description: Stanford PFI 10-item composite; 1–5 scale (higher=worse). Corresponds to burnout. Collected at baseline, 6, 12, 18, 24 weeks. Coprimary outcome.
Stanford PFI 6-item subscale; 1–5 scale (higher=better). Coprimary outcome.
Hours/day spent on notes, normalized to 8-hour workday; from EHR audit-log data.
Hours/day documenting outside scheduled hours, normalized to 8-hour workday; EHR audit-log.
Proportion of encounters closed same day or before next patient visit; EHR audit-log.
ICD-10 code alignment with documentation; reviewed by certified coders using standardized rubric. Partial credit for insufficient specificity; zero for billing-validity errors.

Provider Documentation Summarization Quality Instrument 9 (adapted); 5-point scale per domain; LLM-as-judge implementation validated against physician ratings. Unedited AI notes evaluated pre-practitioner review.
Practitioner-reported secondary measure
Practitioner-reported secondary measure
Practitioner-reported secondary measure

keyFindings: Work exhaustion/interpersonal disengagement: −0.44 points (95% CI: −0.62 to −0.25; p<0.001) ✅ SIGNIFICANT

Professional fulfillment: +0.14 points (95% CI: 0.004 to 0.28; p=0.04) — technically significant at 0.04 but pre-specified alpha was 0.025 per coprimary (Bonferroni for 2 primary outcomes). Therefore declared nonsignificant by pre-specified threshold.
Time on notes: −0.36 hours/day (95% CI: −0.55 to −0.17) ✅ SIGNIFICANT — ~21 minutes/day

Work outside work (WoW): −0.50 hours/day (95% CI: −0.90 to −0.09) — significant in primary analysis but sensitive to extreme values; no longer significant after removing top 3% of daily observations. ⚠️ Interpret cautiously
Same-day closure: Not stated as significant in abstract
Diagnostic billing compliance: Improved with ambient AI use (p<0.001) — certified coder review showed better ICD-10 alignment with documentation
PDSQI-9 note quality: Mean scores 3.97–4.99 across domains on 5-point scale — high quality; no domain fell below 3.97
No drift in software performance detected across 24 weeks
71,487 total notes; 27,092 (38%) ambient AI-generated
3 practitioners discontinued (1 never initiated; 2 due to note template incompatibility in their specialty)
Full data publicly available: https://git.doit.wisc.edu/smph-public/LearningHealthSystem/ambientlistening

id: Albrecht 2025 - Ambient AI Clinical Documentation QI Survey
title: Enhancing clinical documentation with ambient artificial intelligence: a quality improvement survey assessing clinician perspectives on work burden, burnout, and job satisfaction
authors: Albrecht, Michael; Shanks, Denton; Shah, Tina; Hudson, Taina; Thompson, Jeffrey; Filardi, Tanya; Wright, Kelli; Ator, Gregory A.; Smith, Timothy Ryan
journal: JAMIA Open | Vol. 8, No. 1 | 2025. Open Access. University of Kansas Medical Center (KUMC). Abridge-affiliated co-author (Tina Shah). Received July 11, 2024; Accepted February 7, 2025.
product: **Abridge** (explicitly named). Ambient AI documentation platform. iPhone/Android app records conversations; generates structured notes that can be edited and inserted into Epic via dot phrases. Partial integration during study (not full Epic mobile app to note automation). 30 specialties represented; grouped into primary care, medical subspecialty, surgical subspecialty for analysis.
setting: University of Kansas Medical Center (KUMC). 181 clinicians initially offered access; 133 "adopters" (completed ≥5 encounters) offered post-survey. **Pre-survey:** 93/181 (51.9% response). **Post-survey:** 99/133 (74.4% response). Pre-survey anonymous; post-survey not. Median days using Abridge before post-survey: 92 days (IQR 66-172). 87% physicians, 13% APPs. Demographics of post-survey respondents: median age 41, median years in practice 12, median years at institution 4.
evidenceLevel: Quality Improvement (pre/post survey study)
dataSources: Pre/post surveys (14 items total across both surveys). Pre: 8 items on baseline documentation experience. Post: 11 items on perceived impact. Several concordant questions analyzed together using proportional odds logistic regression (POLR). Covariate analysis for specialty type and days of use.
summary: This QI study at KUMC assessed clinician perceptions before and after implementing Abridge ambient AI documentation across 30 specialties. Key quantitative findings: clinicians had **6.91 times higher odds** of reporting easier documentation workflow post-implementation (95% CI 3.90-12.56, p<.001) and **4.95 times higher odds** of agreeing they could complete notes before the next patient (95% CI 2.87-8.69, p<.001). In the post-survey, 81% agreed the tool made workflow easy, 77% reported decreased documentation burden, 73% reported decreased after-hours documentation, 67% reported reduced burnout risk, and 64% reported increased work satisfaction. 47% agreed they could add at least one more patient if needed. Specialty type and days of Abridge use did not significantly affect responses. Pre-survey showed high baseline documentation burden (83% spent time outside clinical hours, 75% at risk for burnout). The study is notable for including medical and surgical subspecialists (not just primary care) and showing consistent benefit across specialties.
hypothesis: To evaluate the impact of Abridge ambient AI documentation on clinicians’ perceptions of documentation burden, after-hours work, burnout risk, and job satisfaction.

measures.description: Concordant question: "I find the documentation workflow easy." Post-implementation: OR 6.91 [3.90-12.56], p<.001. Clinicians 7× more likely to agree workflow is easy with Abridge.
measures.dimensions: US - User Satisfaction, II - Individual Impact

measures.description: "I can complete the note before the next patient visit." Post-implementation: OR 4.95 [2.87-8.69], p<.001. 5× higher odds post-Abridge.
Post-survey: 77% agreed/strongly agreed Abridge decreased documentation burden.
Post-survey: 73% agreed/strongly agreed Abridge decreased time spent documenting outside clinical hours.
Post-survey: 67% agreed/strongly agreed Abridge reduced risk for burnout due to documentation.
Post-survey: 64% agreed/strongly agreed Abridge increased satisfaction at work.
Post-survey: 47% agreed/strongly agreed at least one more patient could be added to clinic sessions if needed.
Pre-survey: 83% spent time documenting outside clinical hours; 75% felt at risk for burnout due to documentation.
measures.dimensions: II - Individual Impact, US - User Satisfaction

measures.description: No significant difference in post-survey responses by specialty type (primary care, medical subspecialty, surgical subspecialty). Benefit consistent across specialties.
No significant effect of number of days using Abridge on post-survey responses. Benefit seen quickly.
DOI: 10.1093/jamiaopen/ooaf013

id: Anderson 2025 - Quality Safety Ambient Digital Scribe Platforms Simulation
title: Evaluating the Quality and Safety of Ambient Digital Scribe Platforms Using Simulated Ambulatory Encounters
authors: Anderson, Taylor N.; Mohan, Vishnu; Dorr, David A.; Ratwani, Raj M.; Biro, Joshua M.; Gold, Jeffrey A.
journal: Mayo Clinic Proceedings: Digital Health | Vol. 3, No. 4 | December 2025 | Article 100292. Open Access (CC BY 4.0). Oregon Health & Science University (OHSU) / MedStar Health National Center for Human Factors in Healthcare. Note: Joshua M. Biro and Raj M. Ratwani are also co-authors of Biro 2025 (Paper 24) — same MedStar/simulation testing group.
product: **Five anonymized ADS platforms (A–E)** — 4 commercially available (A–D) + 1 free platform (E). Products not named. Evaluated December 2024–February 2025 using most current platform version at time of evaluation. This is a methodological/comparative study; product names deliberately withheld.
setting: Simulation study — no real patients. 14 simulated ambulatory clinical encounters from a pre-existing medical scribe training curriculum. Audio played from MacBook Pro laptop, captured by ADS app on iPhone 14 Pro at ~1 ft distance in quiet room. Encounters cover a range of ambulatory specialties (Supplemental Table 1). Mean encounter duration: 13.4 minutes (range 7.9–22.1 min). Professional human transcription used as gold standard.
evidenceLevel: Observational (simulation testing — technical evaluation)
dataSources: Simulation data (ADS-generated transcripts vs. professional transcriptions) | Clinical note rubric scoring (key element accuracy) | PDQI-9 note quality assessment | AHRQ harm scale (adapted for potential risk)
summary: This simulation study from OHSU and MedStar evaluated 5 ambient digital scribe platforms across 14 standardized clinical scenarios, assessing transcript accuracy, clinical note quality (PDQI-9), note accuracy/completeness (rubric-based), and clinical harm potential (AHRQ scale). Key findings: transcripts from platforms A–D averaged 13.9 errors/case; 19.5% of transcript errors were transmitted to the clinical note. Mean clinical note error rate across platforms was **26.3%** of key elements — more than 1 in 4 key clinical elements omitted or incorrect. **Omissions** were the dominant error type (76.3% of all errors), significantly more common than commission errors — consistent with Biro 2025 and contradicting the "hallucination = primary concern" assumption. Mean of **3.0 errors per case had potential for moderate-to-severe harm** (AHRQ ≥2). Platform E (free) was substantially worse than commercial platforms on all metrics. Only 35.8% of correctly reported elements were consistently correct across all platforms — highlighting intra-corpus variability. PDQI-9 mean score was 36±4, with significant variation between platforms (C and B best; E worst). Authors conclude there is urgent need for standardized, objective evaluation frameworks for ADS before widespread clinical adoption.
hypothesis: Explicit: to evaluate and compare the quality and safety of ambient digital scribe platforms using simulated ambulatory encounters.

measures.description: Errors in ASR transcript vs. professional transcription. Mean 13.9 errors/case (95% CI 6.0–17.5) across platforms A–D. Platforms C and D significantly worse than A and B (p<.0020). Platform E did not produce transcripts (edited summaries only).
measures.dimensions: SQ - System Quality, IQ - Information Quality

measures.description: Proportion of transcript errors that appeared in the clinical note. Mean 19.5% (95% CI 6.6–28.8%). No significant difference between platforms A–D.
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, SQ - System Quality

measures.measure: Clinical Note Error Rate (% key elements)
measures.description: Percentage of key clinical elements omitted or captured erroneously. Mean 26.3% (95% CI 17.0–31.0%) across all platforms. Platform E significantly worse (46.1% error rate vs. 16.7–23.3% for A–D).

Most common error type: 76.3% of all errors were omissions (95% CI 70.0–83.3%). Significantly more than commission errors (p=.022). Consistent with Biro 2025 finding. Platform E had higher commission rate than C.

Mean 3.0 errors per case (range 0–21) had potential for moderate-to-severe harm (AHRQ ≥2). Platform E significantly worse (weighted harm score higher than A–D, p<.0082 for all). Platforms A–D not significantly different from each other.
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, OI - Organizational Impact

measures.description: Only 35.8% ± 11.3% of correctly reported elements were consistently correct across all 5 platforms. High variability means a physician can't reliably predict which elements will be accurate for any given case.
measures.dimensions: SQ - System Quality, IQ - Information Quality

measures.description: Mean 36±4 across all platforms. Platform C highest (39.4), B second (38.4), E lowest (32.3). C > A, D, E (p<.028); B > E (p=.001). Significant inter-platform variation.
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, SQ - System Quality

measures.description: Mean 444±120 words across A–D. Platform E significantly shorter (271 words) than B, C, D (p<.001). Shorter + higher error rate for E.

id: Arko 2025 - Documenting Care AI Comparative Commercial Scribe Tools
title: Documenting Care with AI: A Comparative Analysis of Commercial Scribe Tools
authors: Arko IV, Leopold; Hudelson, Carly; Kumar, Jaya; Badlani, Sameer; Stoffel, Michelle; Markowitz, Rebecca; Melton, Genevieve B.
journal: Studies in Health Technology and Informatics (SHTI) | MEDINFO 2025 — Healthcare Smart × Medicine Deep | pp. 337–341. Open Access (CC BY-NC 4.0). University of Minnesota / M Health Fairview Systems, Minneapolis MN.
product: **Four anonymized commercially available AI scribe solutions** (Vendor A, B, C, D). Selected based on commercial availability at time of study. No product names disclosed. No specific prompting prior to encounters — tested "out of the box."
setting: Simulation study — no real patients. Two simulated patient scenarios: (1) new atrial fibrillation in an outpatient medical patient; (2) new headache in a neurosurgical patient. Two physician reviewers: board-certified pediatric neurosurgeon and clinical informatics fellow (L.A.); board-certified general internist with clinical documentation improvement expertise (C.H.). Same standardized audio recordings used for all 4 vendors. Checklist of expected data elements created per encounter (14–27 elements per encounter). Friedman test for between-vendor comparisons. University of Minnesota.
evidenceLevel: Observational (simulation study — comparative note quality evaluation)
dataSources: Error taxonomy (omission, addition, incorrect facts) vs. checklist of expected elements | PDQI-9 note quality (consensus scoring) | Note length ratio
summary: This small simulation study from University of Minnesota/M Health Fairview compared 4 commercial AI scribe tools on 2 outpatient scenarios (internal medicine + neurosurgical). Error taxonomy and PDQI-9 used as primary metrics. Overall error rate: **18.9%** (range 12.2–24.4% across vendors). No statistically significant differences between vendors on any measure (p=0.345 for error rate; p=0.267 for PDQI-9; p=0.112 for note length ratio) — but the study was severely underpowered with only 2 encounters. Omissions dominated at 71% of errors (consistent with Biro 2025 and Anderson 2025). Vendor A had lowest error rate (12.2%) and balanced note length. Vendor B had highest addition errors (7.3%) and lowest PDQI-9 (35.5), especially poor on succinctness (mean 2/5). Vendor C had highest omission rate (24.4%) but shortest notes and highest PDQI-9 (39.5). Vendor D was the only one with incorrect fact errors (3.7%). Authors acknowledge the study is limited by only 2 cases and plan larger follow-up with harm severity scale.
hypothesis: To evaluate quality and accuracy of notes generated by 4 commercial AI scribes using simulated encounters and create a framework for institutional vendor evaluation.

measures.description: Errors / total expected data elements. Mean 18.9%; range 12.2–24.4%. No significant difference (p=0.345). Vendor A lowest (12.2%), Vendor C highest (24.4%).
Missing expected data elements. 71% of all errors. Vendor C highest omission rate (24.4%) — short notes = more omissions.
Elements added but not discussed in encounter. 19.4% of errors. Vendors B and D only. Vendor B highest (7.3%).
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, SQ - System Quality

measures.description: Factually wrong transcriptions. Only 6.5% of errors. Only Vendor D had incorrect facts (3.7%).
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, SQ - System Quality

measures.description: Vendor A: 38.5, B: 35.5, C: 39.5, D: 39.5. No significant difference (p=0.267). Vendor B lowest; Vendor C and D highest.
Vendor B mean 2.0/5 — below 3.5 threshold. Only individual PDQI-9 element with a notably low score. Not statistically significant (p=0.145).
Words per note / number of expected data elements. Range 4.6–17.7 across vendors (p=0.112). Vendor B longest (17.7), Vendor C shortest (4.6).

id: Baker 2023 - ChatGPT Ability Assist Clinical Documentation Orthopaedic RCT
title: ChatGPT’s Ability to Assist with Clinical Documentation: A Randomized Controlled Trial
authors: Baker, Hayden P.; Dwyer, Emma; Kalidoss, Senthooran; Hynes, Kelly; Wolf, Jennifer; Strelzow, Jason A.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (JAAOS) | Vol. 32, No. 3 | February 1, 2024 | pp. 123–129. Orthopaedic-specific study. University of Chicago. Randomized controlled pilot study.
product: **ChatGPT** (GPT-4, OpenAI) — used as a clinical documentation assistant. Not a dedicated ambient AI scribe like DAX or Abridge. Standardized prompt: “Please write a patient history of present illness including the following points:” followed by pertinent history points.
setting: University of Chicago, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery. **11 participants**: 4 medical students, 4 orthopaedic surgery residents (2 PGY3, 2 PGY4), 3 attending surgeons. 10 standardized patient histories with common orthopaedic conditions. Blinded, randomized controlled pilot study. Participants randomized into cohorts based on documentation technique (typing, dictation, ChatGPT). Each participant completed 3 HPIs using a different method for each. Time limit: 5 minutes to ask and collect details. 33 HPIs available for review.
evidenceLevel: RCT (blinded, randomized controlled pilot study)
dataSources: Direct time measurement | Word count | History items included | PDQI-9 (9-item Physician Documentation Quality Instrument, 0–45) | Global rating tool (1–5) | Error/hallucination detection
summary: This orthopaedic-specific RCT is one of the few randomized controlled studies in the corpus. 11 participants (students, residents, attendings) documented 3 HPIs each using typing, dictation, and ChatGPT. Key findings: (1) **Dictation fastest** (43s), ChatGPT intermediate (69s), typing slowest (97s); (2) **ChatGPT produced significantly longer notes** (136 words vs. 90 dictation, 68 typing); (3) **ChatGPT had highest PDQI-9 and overall rating scores** — significantly better than typing and dictation; (4) ChatGPT included **erroneous/fictitious history points in 36% of documents** (4/11); (5) Poor inter-rater agreement on PDQI-9 and global rating between reviewers (ICC 0.1–0.19). Efficiency score (PDQI-9 per minute): dictation 51.1, ChatGPT 34.9, typing 21.5. Authors conclude ChatGPT has potential to improve clinical documentation by producing more comprehensive and organized HPIs, but additional studies are needed to address privacy, bias, and accuracy concerns. Hallucination rate of 36% is a significant safety signal.
hypothesis: To investigate ChatGPT’s ability to assist with writing a history of present illness based on standardized patient histories compared with histories generated solely by healthcare professionals.

measures.description: Dictation fastest (43s), ChatGPT intermediate (69s), typing slowest (97s). Dictation significantly faster than typing (p=0.001). ChatGPT not significantly different from either.
ChatGPT significantly longer (136 words) than dictation (90) and typing (68). p≤0.001 for both comparisons.
ChatGPT significantly higher than typing (p=0.001) and dictation (p=0.01). Faculty scored highest overall (34.2), then residents (32.1), students (31.8).
ChatGPT significantly higher than typing (p=0.008) and dictation (p=0.04).
ChatGPT included significantly more history items (6 vs. 5 for both dictation and typing, p=0.03).
ChatGPT included erroneous/fictitious history points in **36%** (4/11) documents. Hallucinations included adding physical exam findings and patient history details not in standardized script.
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, SQ - System Quality

measures.description: Dictation 51.1, ChatGPT 34.9, typing 21.5. Dictation significantly better than typing (p≤0.001). No significant difference between dictation and ChatGPT (p=0.06).
Poor agreement on PDQI-9 (ICC 0.1) and overall rating (ICC 0.19).

id: Balloch 2024 - Ambient Artificial Intelligence Tool Improve Quality Clinical Documentation
title: Use of an ambient artificial intelligence tool to improve quality of clinical documentation
authors: Balloch, Jasmine; Sridharan, Shankar; Oldham, Geralyn; Wray, Jo; Gough, Paul; Robinson, Robert; Sebire, Neil J.; Khalil, Saleh; Asgari, Elham; Tan, Christopher; Taylor, Andrew; Pimenta, Dominic
journal: Future Healthcare Journal | Vol. 11, Issue 3 | September 2024 | 100157. Open Access. Future Healthcare Journal (Royal College of Physicians). Simulation study.
product: Ambient AI tool (unnamed in abstract — likely DAX or similar). Used in simulated outpatient consultations with actors and clinicians.
setting: Simulation study — outpatient consultations simulated with actors and clinicians. Documentation assessed using Sheffield Assessment Instrument for Letters (SAIL). Clinician experience measured through questionnaires and NASA Task Load Index. No real patients.
evidenceLevel: Observational (simulation study)
dataSources: Simulated consultations | SAIL for documentation quality | Questionnaires and NASA Task Load Index for clinician experience
summary: This simulation study evaluated an ambient AI tool's impact on clinical documentation quality and clinician experience. AI-produced documentation achieved higher SAIL scores than standard EHR practices. Consultations were 26.3% shorter on average without impacting patient interaction time. Clinicians reported an enhanced experience and reduced task load. The study concludes that the AI tool significantly improved documentation quality and operational efficiency in simulated consultations. Clinicians recognized its potential to improve note-taking processes.
hypothesis: To assess the clinical utility of an ambient AI tool in enhancing consultation experience and the completion of clinical documentation.

measures.description: Sheffield Assessment Instrument for Letters for documentation quality. AI-produced documentation achieved higher SAIL scores.
AI consultations 26.3% shorter on average without impacting patient interaction time.
Enhanced experience and reduced task load reported by clinicians (NASA Task Load Index).

id: Billings 2025a - Ambient AI Assistant Transforms Primary Care Experience
title: Ambient AI Assistant Transforms the Primary Care Experience
authors: Billings, Edmund MD; Waldren, Steven E. MD MS (Phyx Primary Care)
doi: None — grey literature (Phyx Primary Care Innovation Lab report; not peer-reviewed)
journal: Phyx Primary Care Report | February 2025
product: **Suki AI** — described by Phyx as an "AI Clinical Assistant" (explicitly distinguished from "Ambient AI Scribes" which Phyx frames as simpler/less integrated products). Phyx selected Suki after assessing companies in the category; chosen for voice/AI technology, mission alignment, primary care focus, scalability, safety compliance, and EHR integration capability.
setting: Primary care — 116 primary care physicians surveyed across 37 practice locations nationwide. Mix of small independent practices, primary care organizations, and large health systems. ~80% small/independent. All respondents had used Suki AI Assistant for **more than 30 days** prior to survey.
evidenceLevel: Grey Literature (non-profit innovation lab report; survey-based; not peer-reviewed)
dataSources: Survey/self-report — Phyx Primary Care Vital Signs® (proprietary validated instrument); online survey; Net Promoter Score; 3 open-ended qualitative questions
summary: Phyx Primary Care (nonprofit innovation lab) evaluated Suki AI's impact on 116 primary care physicians across 37 locations using their proprietary Primary Care Vital Signs® survey instrument. All respondents had used Suki for >30 days. The survey assessed burnout, practice satisfaction, scheduled visit time, adequacy of patient care time, and after-hours EHR time. Key findings: 60% fewer physicians reporting burnout; 81% more reported practice satisfaction; 41% reduction in notetaking time; 27% reduction in documentation burden; 44% reported improved patient interactions "to a great extent"; 20% could increase patient care time during visit; 33% spent less time after-hours. Report frames Suki as an "AI Clinical Assistant" vs. simpler "Ambient AI Scribes."

id: Biro 2025 - Simulation Testing Ambient Digital Scribes Case Report
title: The value of simulation testing for the evaluation of ambient digital scribes: a case report
authors: Biro, Joshua M.; Handley, Jessica L.; Mickler, James; Reddy, Sahithi; Kottamasu, Varsha; Ratwani, Raj M.; Cobb, Nathan K.
journal: Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association (JAMIA) | Vol. 32, No. 5 | 2025 | pp. 928–931. Case Report. Published March 21, 2025. MedStar Health National Center for Human Factors in Healthcare / Georgetown University.
product: **Two anonymized Ambient Digital Scribe (ADS) products** (referred to as ADS Product A and ADS Product B). The study evaluates commercial ADS products without naming vendors — deliberate anonymization for the case report. Products use LLMs to capture patient-provider conversations and generate structured clinical notes.
setting: MedStar Health (large Mid-Atlantic healthcare system). Simulation study — not real patient encounters. 11 real outpatient encounter recordings were transcribed and edited into dialogue scripts covering 10 service lines (Otolaryngology, Cardiology, Rheumatology, Family Medicine, Pediatrics, Endocrinology, Internal Medicine, Gastroenterology, Oncology, Urgent Care). Two researcher-physician teams (2 physicians, 2 researchers) simulated reading the scripts while both ADS products listened. 44 draft notes generated (11 scripts × 2 teams × 2 products). IRB approved.
evidenceLevel: Observational (simulation testing case report — rapid evaluation methodology)
dataSources: Simulation data (editing time, Likert-scale ratings of editing effort, error classification)
summary: This case report from MedStar Health demonstrates the value of **simulation testing** as a rapid method for evaluating ambient digital scribe products. Using 11 scripted outpatient encounters read by researcher-physician teams, the study compared two commercial ADS products across 44 draft notes. Key findings: ADS Product A took significantly longer to edit (80.6 seconds longer, p<.001) but had fewer omissions and more additions/irrelevant text errors than Product B. Physicians rated ADS Product A as performing better for most encounters (12/22), despite longer editing time. Error analysis: omissions were the most common error type across both products (54.1% for A, 83.3% for B). The authors argue that simulation testing is a crucial first step in evaluating AI-enabled technologies because it can identify safety and usability issues in hours rather than months, allowing healthcare systems to efficiently screen products before pilot implementation. They position simulation testing as complementary to pilot testing — simulation for underlying technology/interface issues, pilot for workflow integration.
hypothesis: Explicit: to demonstrate the value of simulation testing for rapidly evaluating artificial intelligence products (specifically ADS).

title: Ambient AI reduces documentation time and enhances quality in a simulated inpatient setting
authors: Bracken, Aisling; Babu, Anita Rose; Whelehan, Seán; Merghani, Khalid; Sheehan, Eoin; Feeley, Iain
journal: The Surgeon | Vol. 24, Issue 2 | April 2026 | pp. 119–125. Royal College of Surgeons of Edinburgh / Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Received September 15, 2025; Accepted October 28, 2025. Irish study — RCSI / Midlands Regional Hospital Tullamore / University of Limerick.
product: **Heidi Health** (Melbourne, Australia) — a general-purpose ambient AI documentation platform compliant with GDPR, HIPAA, and NHS standards. A customized progress note and discharge summary template was configured for the orthopaedic service; orthopaedic terminology added to Heidi's "memory bank." This is one of very few papers in the corpus evaluating Heidi Health specifically.
setting: Simulation study — no real patients. Clinical skills and simulation laboratory at Midlands Regional Hospital Tullamore (Ireland). 7 postgraduate year-1 (PGY-1) junior doctors (interns) with orthopaedic ward experience. 15 mock inpatient cases simulating orthopaedic trauma, infection, and elective procedures (admission 1–7 days). Background noise deliberately introduced to replicate ward environment. 150 total clinical documents: 120 progress notes (60 manual retrospective, 60 AI real-time) + 30 discharge summaries (15 manual, 15 AI). Two independent blinded PDQI-9 reviewers; consensus resolution with third author.
evidenceLevel: Observational (simulation study — within-subjects comparison)
dataSources: Direct time measurement (seconds) | PDQI-9 note quality assessment (blinded) | NASA-TLX workload assessment (pre/post)
summary: This Irish simulation study is the **only paper in the corpus focusing on inpatient ward round documentation** (all other papers examine ambulatory/outpatient settings). 7 junior doctors participated in simulated orthopaedic ward rounds using Heidi Health ambient AI, generating 150 documents across progress notes and discharge summaries. Results: ambient AI reduced progress note time by **78.9%** (median 27s vs. 128s, p<.0001; large effect r=0.87) and discharge summary time by **75%** (median 114s vs. 459s, p<.0001). Time savings scaled with complexity — the more complex the case, the greater the absolute time saving. Documentation quality improved significantly: progress notes PDQI-9 median 43.5 vs. 41 (p=.002); discharge summaries 40 vs. 33 (p<.0001). Crucially, **no significant difference in accuracy** for either document type — quality gains were in thoroughness, currency, usefulness, comprehensibility, organization, internal consistency, and synthesis rather than accuracy. 55% of AI notes required no edits at all; 40% needed minor formatting fixes; only 3% (2/60) had clinically significant errors. NASA-TLX: all 6 domains improved, with largest gains in frustration (−79%) and effort (−81%).

id: Bundy 2024 - Administrative Loads Physicians AI Documentation Qualitative
title: Can the Administrative Loads of Physicians be Alleviated by AI-Facilitated Clinical Documentation?
authors: Bundy, Henry; Gerhart, Jay; Baek, Sally; Connor, C.; Isler, M.; et al.
journal: Journal of General Internal Medicine | Vol. 39 | pp. 2995–3000 | 2024. Open Access. Atrium Health / Wake Forest University Health Sciences. Published June 27, 2024.
product: **DAX Copilot (DAXC)** — Nuance/Microsoft. GPT-4 powered generative AI clinical documentation tool. EHR-integrated version generating narrative note summaries within 30 seconds of encounter completion. Pilot at Atrium Health (multi-site academic learning health system). ClinicalTrials.gov: NCT06329427. Funded by Wake Forest University Health Sciences.
setting: Atrium Health — multi-site academic learning health system. 116 primary care physicians recruited to pilot DAXC. Purposive sample of 12 interviewees selected for diversity (early-career, women, minority providers overrepresented due to higher burnout risk). Semi-structured interviews August–September 2023. Interviews ~30 min, conducted via Microsoft Teams. Saturation reached after 10th interview.
evidenceLevel: Qualitative (semi-structured interviews, inductive thematic analysis)
dataSources: Semi-structured interviews (n=12 physicians) | Qualitative thematic analysis (ATLAS.ti v.9)
summary: This qualitative study at Atrium Health explored 12 primary care physicians' experiences with DAX Copilot in an early pilot via semi-structured interviews. Three major themes emerged: (1) **Benefits of DAXC** — reduced documentation time, cognitive burden relief ("cognitive offloading"), more personable patient encounters, more eye contact, reduced fear of losing clinical details; (2) **Suitable encounters** — physicians disagreed on which encounter types work best; some preferred DAXC for complex/protracted visits, others for simple acute visits, but all found edge cases where DAXC fell short; (3) **Concerns** — verbosity/inappropriately long notes (six paragraphs for a common cold), misgendering patients, hallucinations (fabricating additional patients in the room, imagined events, wrong diagnoses), negation errors ("patient did NOT" transcribed as "patient did"), verbatim/chronologically disordered narrative. A key novel concern: several physicians (n=7) worried that DAXC efficiency would be used to justify seeing more patients — framing efficiency gains as potentially threatening autonomy and work-life balance. Atrium Health subsequently expanded to 2,500 DAXC licenses.
hypothesis: Explicit: to evaluate physicians' experiences with DAX Copilot and assess its impact on workflows, workloads, and how it could most effectively be incorporated into practice.

id: Castro 2025 - Psychiatric Documentation Treatment Primary Care AI Scribe
title: Changes in psychiatric documentation and treatment in primary care with artificial intelligence scribe use
authors: Castro, Victor M.; McCoy, Thomas H.; Verhaak, Pilar; Ramachandiran, Anudeepa K.; Perlis, Roy H.
journal: medRxiv preprint (NOT peer-reviewed). Posted May 15, 2025. Center for Quantitative Health and Department of Psychiatry, Massachusetts General Hospital / Harvard Medical School. License: CC0 (Public Domain).
product: AI-driven ambient scribes (not named by product). Deployed at Mass General and Brigham and Women's Hospital systems February 2023 onward. Same institution as Prasad 2025 (MGB), likely same deployment (DAX-Nuance or similar based on MGB rollout timing).
setting: Massachusetts General and Brigham and Women's Hospital systems. Primary care annual visit notes, February 2023–February 2024. Case-control EHR study: **n=20,302 notes** matched 1:1 using sociodemographic and clinical features across 4 groups: (1) ambient scribe, (2) human scribe, (3) same period no scribe, (4) pre-deployment no scribe. GPT-4o (HIPAA-compliant) used to extract neuropsychiatric symptoms from notes. IRB approved with waiver of informed consent.
evidenceLevel: Observational (case-control EHR study)
dataSources: EHR data (clinical notes, NLP/LLM extraction of psychiatric symptoms and interventions) | Administrative data (diagnostic codes, prescriptions, referrals)
summary: This large case-control EHR study at MGH/BWH examined whether AI ambient scribe use changes how primary care physicians document and act on psychiatric symptoms in annual wellness visits. Using GPT-4o to extract Research Domain Criteria (RDoC) symptomatology from 20,302 matched notes, the study found a striking paradox: **ambient-scribed notes documented significantly more neuropsychiatric symptoms** in all 6 RDoC domains (p<.001 for all) compared to controls, yet **likelihood of a psychiatric intervention was significantly lower** among ambient-scribed visits (aOR 0.83 [0.72–0.95], p=.005). Human-scribed visits showed no such difference from unscribed (aOR 1.01 [0.87–1.17], p=NS). The interpretation: ambient scribes may be capturing more symptom language but this additional documentation is not translating to clinical action — and may even be displacing it. Authors call for further investigation into whether this represents a documentation artifact, a cognitive load effect, or a genuine change in clinical behavior that could affect patient outcomes. This is the **only paper in the corpus examining downstream patient-care outcomes** rather than clinician experience.
hypothesis: Explicit: to characterize differences in documentation and treatment of psychiatric symptoms in primary care outpatient notes generated using ambient scribes.

5.0% of ambient-scribed visits met criteria for moderate or greater depressive symptoms by PHQ-9. Used as baseline severity context.
1:1 matching on age, sex, sociodemographic factors, and clinical features across 4 groups. Ambient scribe group: mean age 48 (SD 14); 59% female.

GPT-4o used to extract RDoC symptom levels from de-identified notes. Represents a novel outcome measure methodology — LLM-as-evaluator of clinical note content. Accuracy/validation of GPT-4o extraction not reported in abstract.

summary: This mixed-methods study at Penn Medicine evaluated DAX Copilot across 46 ambulatory clinicians, using both EHR telemetry and detailed survey instruments. id: Evans 2025 - AI Scribe Allied Health Private Practice Australia
title: Impact of using an AI scribe on clinical documentation and clinician-patient interactions in allied health private practice: perspectives of clinicians and patients
authors: Evans, Kerrie; Papinniemi, Amy; Ploderer, Bernd; Nicholson, Vaughan; Hindhaugh, Tom; Vuvan, Viana; Cowley, Nicholas; Tariq, Amina; Thomson, Hayley
journal: Musculoskeletal Science and Practice | Vol. 78 | August 2025 | Article 103333. Open Access (CC BY 4.0). University of Sydney / University of Queensland / Queensland University of Technology / Australian Catholic University / Healthia Limited.
product: **Lyrebird Health** (Lyrebird) — AI scribe product. Transcribes AHP-patient conversations, then generates consultation notes, referral letters, and other clinical documentation. AHPs review and edit before transferring to practice management software. Not integrated directly with the EHR. Product had been piloted by 8 clinicians in the 6 months prior to the study.
setting: Australian allied health private practice within **Healthia Limited** — a privately owned allied healthcare company operating on a partnership model. 876 full-time AHPs in the organization; 119 participated. Physiotherapists (76%), podiatrists (10%), occupational therapists (8%), exercise physiologists (5%). 75% metropolitan, 23% regional, 2% rural. 46% female. Study period: February–July 2024. Mixed-methods: AHP surveys at baseline, 6 weeks, 3 months; patient survey once after AI-scribed appointment; semi-structured interviews with 15 AHPs + 14 patients after 3 months.
evidenceLevel: Observational (longitudinal mixed-methods QI study — quantitative surveys + semi-structured interviews)
dataSources: AHP surveys (baseline, 6 weeks, 3 months) | Patient surveys (cross-sectional, one time) | Semi-structured interviews (15 AHPs, 14 patients) | Productivity data from practice management software
summary: This is the only study in the corpus examining an AI scribe in **allied health** settings (physiotherapy, podiatry, OT, exercise physiology) and the only Australian private practice study. 119 AHPs and 157 patients participated across a large private practice network over 3 months. Quantitative results: significant reduction in time spent on notes, letters, and out-of-hours documentation between baseline and 6 weeks, and baseline and 3 months — but no further change between 6 and 3 months, suggesting benefits plateau quickly. Documentation burden significantly less at 3 months vs. baseline (Cohen's d = 0.22). Productivity increased by an average of **5.8%**. 95% of AHPs at 3 months agreed or strongly agreed they would recommend continued AI scribe use. Job satisfaction was mixed — only 29% agreed AI improved job satisfaction (23% somewhat agreed). Patient perspective: comfortable consenting to AI scribe; some noted other patients might need more information about data storage. Qualitative themes: positive impact on therapeutic alliance and administrative workload; trust facilitated uptake; AHPs valued scribe for different aspects of documentation. Notable: patient opt-outs during 3-month period totaled only 74 out of an estimated 64,099 appointments — extremely low refusal rate.
hypothesis: Explicit: to describe perspectives and experiences of AHPs and patients regarding the use of an AI scribe for clinical documentation in Australian allied health private practice.

id: Falcetta 2023 - Automatic Documentation Professional Health Interactions Systematic Review
title: Automatic documentation of professional health interactions: A systematic review
authors: Falcetta, Frederico Soares; de Almeida, Fernando Kude; Lemos, Janaína Conceição Sutil; Goldim, José Roberto; da Costa, Cristiano André
journal: Artificial Intelligence in Medicine | Vol. 137 | March 2023 | 102487. Elsevier. Systematic review. Brazilian research group (Unisinos, Hospital Fêmina, Hospital de Clínicas de Porto Alegre).
product: N/A — systematic review. The review searched for intelligent solutions for automatic speech recognition (ASR) with simultaneous structured documentation during a medical interview. Excluded speech-to-text-only technologies. 1995 titles screened → 8 studies included. Most used neural networks, medical lexicon, and structured text output. No commercially available products at time of publication (2023). Limited real-life experience reported. None prospectively validated in large-scale clinical studies.
setting: Systematic review — not a primary study. Included studies from multiple countries (no geographic restriction). Focused on clinical (doctor-patient) interactions, not radiology reports or other non-interactive documentation. The review highlights the complexity of medical vocabulary, context understanding, non-linear conversation flow, and room noise as major technical challenges.
evidenceLevel: Systematic Review
dataSources: Ovid MEDLINE, Embase, Web of Science, Cochrane, PubMed Central (2014–2022). PRISMA-like methodology. Included only original research on systems that transcribe speech in natural and structured fashion simultaneously with the doctor-patient interaction.
summary: This Brazilian systematic review is one of the most comprehensive methodological syntheses in the corpus. 1,995 titles screened → 8 primary studies met inclusion criteria (real-time ASR + structured documentation during medical interview). Key conclusions: (1) No commercially available product existed at time of publication (2023); (2) None had large-scale prospective validation or real-world testing; (3) Intelligent models primarily consisted of ASR + NLP + medical lexicon + structured output; (4) Major technical challenges include medical vocabulary complexity, context understanding, non-linear conversation flow, room noise, and speaker variability; (5) Ethical, legal, and privacy concerns are major barriers. The review is explicitly cautious — "clinical data on the usability and benefits of such applications is almost non-existent" and "future work in this area is necessary." This paper directly informs the Regenstrief/Suki evaluation framework by identifying the methodological gaps that must be addressed.
hypothesis: To establish the current model and technology status for the automatic transcribing of healthcare professionals’ patient interactions.

measures.description: 1,995 titles → 8 included studies. Extremely low yield highlights the nascent state of the field.
None of the 8 included studies had large-scale prospective validation or real-world testing. All were early technical demonstrations.
None of the included studies had a commercially available product at time of publication (2023).
Medical vocabulary complexity, context understanding, non-linear conversation flow, room noise, speaker variability, data privacy, legal liability.
Privacy, data storage, liability for AI-generated errors, consent for recording. Major barriers identified.

id: Foo 2025 - Great Scribe-Off AI vs Human Documentation Simulated GP
title: The Great Scribe-Off: A Comparative Analysis of AI Scribes Versus Human Documentation in Simulated General Practice Consultations
authors: Foo, Darran; Tan, Janice; Hansra, Amandeep; Stevens, Sean; Wilcox, Helen
journal: Studies in Health Technology and Informatics (SHTI) | Vol. 333 | pp. 34–39 | 2025. Conference proceedings: Health. Innovation. Community: Engage. Disrupt. Transform (HIC2025 — 29th Australasian Institute of Digital Health). Open Access (CC BY-NC 4.0). IOS Press.
product: **Four anonymized commercially available AI scribes accessible in Australia** (Scribe 1, 2, 4, 5) + one human scribe (Scribe 3 = two specialist GPs). AI scribe names withheld per commercial sensitivities and ethics conditions. Tested in default "out-of-the-box" configurations, most up-to-date paid versions. No customization beyond default templates.
setting: Simulation study — no real patients. Four standardised clinical scenarios from the Royal Australian College of General Practitioners (RACGP) examination repository, covering women's health, mental health, urgent care, and chronic disease. Professional or experienced amateur actors played simulated patients. Two specialist GPs (each >5 years experience) conducted 2 consultations each. AI scribes ran on dedicated laptops with omnidirectional microphones. Consultations also video recorded. 20 total clinical notes rated (4 cases × 5 scribes).
evidenceLevel: Observational (simulation study — comparative note quality evaluation)
dataSources: Modified PDQI-9 (10 domains, max 50 per note) rated by 3 blinded experienced GPs | ICC inter-rater reliability | Repeated measures ANOVA
summary: This Australian simulation study compared clinical documentation quality from 4 commercial AI scribes vs. human GP documentation across 4 RACGP exam scenarios, rated by 3 blinded GPs using a modified PDQI-9 (10 domains, max 50). Key finding: AI scribes outperformed human documentation across multiple domains, with top AI scribes scoring mean **44.08/50** vs. **37.42/50** for humans. However, repeated measures ANOVA found no statistically significant difference in overall mean scores (F(4,55)=2.33, p=0.071). Significant domain differences were found in accuracy (p=.049), thoroughness (p=.008), succinctness (p=.029), and freedom from hallucination (p=.025). Humans scored lowest in accuracy, thoroughness, usefulness, comprehensibility, synthesis, and internal consistency. Top performers: Scribe 4 and Scribe 5 (tied at 44.08); Scribe 4 excelled in accuracy/thoroughness but was verbose; Scribe 5 most succinct but slightly less accurate. Scribe 2 had highest hallucination rate. Notably, human notes were not hallucination-free — clinicians extrapolating from context (inferring BMI from weight, specifying tests beyond what was mentioned) were classified as hallucination, highlighting parallels between human cognition and LLM generative behavior. Inter-rater reliability moderate overall (ICC=0.56); good for accuracy and thoroughness; poor for subjective domains like synthesis.
hypothesis: Explicit: to compare documentation quality from commercially available AI scribes and human-generated notes using modified PDQI-9 in simulated general practice consultations.

Significant difference (p=.029). Scribe 5 most succinct; Scribe 4 least succinct. Trade-off between thoroughness and brevity.

id: Guo 2025 - Ambient Listening EPIC Signal Data Physician Workload
title: Ambient Listening in Clinical Practice: Evaluating EPIC Signal Data Before and After Implementation and Its Impact on Physician Workload
authors: Guo, Yawen; Hu, Di; Wang, Jiayuan; Zheng, Kai; Perret, Danielle; Pandita, Deepti; Tam, Steven
doi: No DOI assigned (conference proceedings). IOS Press DOI: 10.3233/SHTI250921. arXiv preprint: 10.48550/arXiv.2504.13879
journal: MEDINFO 2025 — Proceedings of the 20th World Congress on Health and Biomedical Informatics (Healthcare Smart × Medicine Deep), pp. 653–657. IOS Press. Open Access (CC BY-NC 4.0).
product: DAX Copilot (Nuance Communications) and Abridge — both deployed at UCI Health in the same pilot. This analysis treats them collectively (no comparative analysis between tools in this paper).
setting: UCI Health (Orange, California) — multi-specialty outpatient settings including primary care and specialty care (gastroenterology, geriatrics, psychiatry, rheumatology, cardiology, sports medicine, etc.). 124 physicians enrolled; 68 met analysis inclusion criteria (≥4 weeks use, ≥10 notes); final analysis n=42 at 1-month, n=32 at 2-month, n=31 at 3-month post-implementation.
evidenceLevel: Observational (pre/post implementation — quantitative Epic Signal telemetry analysis)
dataSources: EHR data (Epic Signal telemetry — four objective documentation metrics)
summary: This exploratory QI study at UCI Health analyzed Epic Signal data to evaluate the impact of a dual-tool ambient listening pilot (DAX Copilot + Abridge) on physician documentation workload. Using paired two-sample t-tests with a 3-month pre-implementation baseline and up to 3 months of follow-up, four Epic Signal metrics were examined. Key findings: time spent writing a note decreased significantly (6.58 → 5.45 min/note, p<0.001), and total documentation time per provider per day fell from 63.99 to 53.33 min (p<0.001). Paradoxically, note length increased significantly — average characters per note rose by ~559 (p<0.05) and characters per appointment by ~1,021 (p<0.001). The largest changes occurred in month 1, consistent with a learning curve. Authors caution that note length increases may reflect verbose tool-embedded templates rather than clinical enrichment. Only 37% of enrolled physicians met inclusion criteria for analysis — differential adoption is flagged as a key area for future research.

id: Guo 2026 - Evaluating Ambient AI Documentation UCI Health Mixed Methods
title: Evaluating ambient artificial intelligence documentation: effects on work efficiency, documentation burden, and patient-centered care
authors: Guo, Yawen; Wang, Jiayuan; Hu, Di; Tam, Steven; Gilman, Charles; Chow, Emilie; Perret, Danielle; Pandita, Deepti; Zheng, Kai
journal: Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association (JAMIA) | Vol. 33, No. 2 | 2026 | pp. 273–282. Published online October 16, 2025. University of California, Irvine (UCI Health). SQUIRE reporting guidelines. QI study exempt from IRB.
product: **DAX Copilot** (Nuance/Microsoft) and **Abridge** — both deployed at UCI Health and **pooled for analysis** (vendor-specific differences not assessed). Of 65 matched survey respondents: Abridge n=41 (63%), DAX Copilot n=24 (37%). This is the **full mixed-methods follow-up to Guo 2025** (Paper 2 in corpus, MEDINFO conference paper from the same UCI Health pilot — same team, same site, now with 167 EHR metric participants and 65 survey participants over a longer period).
setting: UCI Health (University of California, Irvine) — major academic health system in Southern California (Irvine, Newport Beach, Orange, Fountain Valley). QI pilot launched December 2023; data analyzed December 2023 through April 2025. 319 physicians enrolled. EHR metrics: 167 met inclusion criteria (≥4 weeks tool use, ≥10 notes). Surveys: 65 completed both pre- and post-implementation surveys (~4 months apart). Clinicians from primary care and specialty care; medical trainees included. Epic Haiku mobile app or web browser integration. DAX/Abridge both integrated with Epic.
evidenceLevel: Observational (prospective mixed-methods QI study — EHR telemetry + pre/post survey + thematic analysis)
dataSources: EHR data (Epic Signal — 5 metrics: note length, time per note, daily documentation time, time per appointment, same-day closure rate) | Survey (KLAS Arch Collaborative instrument — Likert + 0–10 scales + open-ended) | Qualitative (thematic analysis of open-ended survey responses)
summary: This mixed-methods QI study at UCI Health is the full follow-up to the MEDINFO conference paper (Guo 2025, Paper 2 in corpus) — significantly expanded with 167 EHR metric participants, 65 matched survey respondents, KLAS Arch Collaborative benchmarked survey, and qualitative thematic analysis. Both DAX Copilot and Abridge pooled (63% Abridge, 37% DAX). EHR metrics: note time decreased from 6.37→5.58 min/note (p<.001), daily doc time 55.97→49.13 min (p<.001), time per appointment 9.91→8.82 min (p<.001). Note length increased modestly (7522→7862 chars, p<.001). **Same-day encounter closure rate did not change significantly** (high baseline ~77%; only physicians with ≥40 encounters/week analyzed, n=26). Benefits stabilized after month 1 — no further improvement at months 2 or 3. Survey (Table 3, 23 questions): 65 matched respondents, 52% primary care, 48% specialty care. Significant improvements across patient safety/care, clinical efficiency, EHR training, and system usability domains. Key documentation burden results: mental demand 6.71→6.11 (p=.031), effort 6.75→6.02 (p=.014); temporal demand (p=.055) and chart completion timing (p=.429) NOT significant. Several significant DiD differences between primary and specialty care: primary care improved more on implementation/training support (DiD=+0.49, p=.035); specialty care improved more on system availability (DiD=−0.59, p=.047). Qualitative themes (Table 4): (1) positive perceptions — reduced burden, enhanced patient focus, improved efficiency; (2) limitations — need for specialty-specific customization, workflow disruptions, accuracy/editing burden; (3) future expectations — inbox management AI, chart summarization, billing automation.
hypothesis: Explicit: ambient AI will improve work efficiency, reduce physician workload, and help mitigate burnout.

id: Haberle 2024 - Impact Nuance DAX Ambient Listening AI Documentation
title: The impact of nuance DAX ambient listening AI documentation: a cohort study
authors: Haberle, Tyler; Cleveland, Courtney; Snow, Greg L.; Barber, Chris; Stookey, Nikki; Thornock, Cari; Younger, Laurie; Mullahkhel, Buzzy; Ize-Ludlow, Diego
journal: Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association (JAMIA) | Vol. 31, No. 4 | 2024 | pp. 975–979. Intermountain Health. Cohort study with peer-matched controls.
product: **Nuance Dragon Ambient eXperience (DAX)**. Ambient listening/digital scribing solution. iOS app captures ambient natural conversation; sent to Microsoft/Nuance cloud for conversational AI parsing; generative AI-derived draft note reviewed by human quality reviewers before being pasted into provider's EHR note for final edit and signature.
setting: Intermountain Health (33-hospital integrated health system across 7 states). Study completed in subset of Intermountain Medical Group clinics in Utah using Oracle Cerner EHR. 99 providers across 12 specialties enrolled; 76 matched control group providers included for analysis. March–September 2022.
evidenceLevel: Observational (peer-matched controlled cohort study)
dataSources: Press Ganey Workforce Engagement surveys | Patient LTR scores | EHR metrics (after-hours EHR time, documentation time per patient) | Documentation and CPT deficiency rates | wRVU productivity | Attributed VBC panel sizes | Patient opt-out counts | Nuance daily turnaround time reports
summary: This cohort study from Intermountain Health evaluated Nuance DAX ambient listening AI documentation across 99 providers representing 12 specialties with 76 matched controls. Median DAX utilization was 47% among active participants. Key findings: positive trends in provider engagement (higher scores in engagement, safety, resilience, and work-life balance compared to all employed adult primary care providers); no quantifiable effect on patient safety or LTR scores; small but statistically significant increase in productivity (wRVU); no meaningful change in VBC-attributed panel sizes; statistically significant worsening of after-hours EHR time; no significant impact on 24-hour documentation or CPT submission deficiency rates. Expert internal audit found no meaningful evidence of over-coding, missed risk-adjustment, or insufficient supporting documentation. Authors note this is the first published study describing the use and practical outcomes of ambient listening AI documentation in real-world outpatient clinical care at modest scale.

authors: Kanaparthy, Naga Sasidhar; Villuendas-Rey, Yenny; Bakare, Tolulope; Diao, Zihan; Iscoe, Mark; Loza, Andrew; Wright, Donald; Safranek, Conrad; Faustino, Isaac V.; Brackett, Alexandria; Melnick, Edward R.; Taylor, R. Andrew

summary: This Yale-led rapid review synthesized real-world evidence on ambient AI digital scribes from 2014–2024, finding only 6 studies that met rigorous inclusion criteria (real-world clinical implementation; not solely technical development or model validation). The four major themes were: (1) **self-reported documentation time decreased** with associated note length increase; (2) **physician burnout measured by validated scales was unaffected** (contrary to popular perception), but physician engagement improved; (3) **physician productivity as measured by billing metrics was unchanged**; (4) **studies fell short when benchmarked against standardized evaluation frameworks** (QUEST and SEIPS 3.0). The review is deliberately skeptical of premature adoption, drawing parallels to robotic surgery and fitness trackers as technologies widely embraced before evidence emerged. Key findings: omissions dominated errors at 86.3%; mean PDQI-9 = 29.7 (closer to "terrible" than "good"); only 53% of data elements consistently correct across all 3 replicates; accuracy inversely correlated with transcript length and information density.

keyFindings: Omissions dominate: 86.3% of all errors were omissions — consistent with simulation findings from Biro 2025, Anderson 2025, Arko 2025, Foo 2025 (71–76% in those papers, this is even higher)

Reproducibility failure: Only 52.9% of data elements reported correctly across all 3 replicates. Nearly 30% appeared in only 1 or 2 replicates. This is a critical reliability concern — the same model makes different errors on the same case each time.
PDQI-9 mean = 29.7: On the validated PDQI-9 scale, 26.2 = "terrible or bad" and 36.6 = "good or excellent." A mean of 29.7 is closer to terrible.
Accuracy by section: Objective section was highest (median 86.9%). HPI was lower (63.8%) and significantly different from Objective (p<.05). Assessment & Plan was lowest (66.9%).
Information density effect: Accuracy inversely correlated with transcript length (p=.05) and number of scorable elements (p=.05). The more complex the case, the worse the model performs.
Not HIPAA-compliant: Open AI platforms cannot be used clinically due to regulatory and privacy issues — authors explicitly flag this.

summary: This is the first randomized-clinical trial of ambient AI scribes, comparing Microsoft DAX and Nabla against a usual-care control across 238 outpatient physicians at UCLA over two months. id: Moramarco 2022 - Human Evaluation Automatic Metrics Consultation Note Generation
title: Human Evaluation and Correlation with Automatic Metrics in Consultation Note Generation
authors: Moramarco, Francesco; Papadopoulos Korfiatis, Alex; Perera, Mark; Juric, Damir; Flann, Jack; Reiter, Ehud; Belz, Anya; Savkov, Aleksandar
journal: arXiv preprint (cs.CL) | April 1, 2022. Published in proceedings of ACL 2022. Authors: Babylon Health (commercial digital health company, UK) + University of Aberdeen. Dataset released as open source: https://github.com/babylonhealth/primock57
product: N/A — NLP/ML evaluation methodology paper. Studies 10 fine-tuned neural summarization models (BART-based) generating the **Subjective section of SOAP notes** from consultation transcripts. Not a commercial ambient AI scribe evaluation — this is a foundational NLP research paper on how to evaluate consultation note generation systems.
setting: Simulation / research setting. 57 mock consultations from the PriMock57 dataset — enacted consultations using clinical case cards covering common primary care topics, ~10 minutes each. Audio transcribed via Google Speech-to-text. 5 practicing UK GPs (≥3 years experience) evaluated notes over 8 weeks (~30 working hours/evaluator). Produced by Babylon Health (commercial AI health company). Consultations span common primary care topics.
evidenceLevel: Simulation (NLP evaluation methodology study — human evaluation + automatic metric correlation)
dataSources: Human evaluation (5 GPs): (1) listen and take notes; (2) post-edit 5 notes per consultation; (3) classify errors (incorrect statements, omissions); (4) qualitative feedback | 18 automatic NLP metrics (ROUGE variants, BertScore, Levenshtein distance, others) | Post-editing time measurement
summary: This foundational NLP methodology paper from Babylon Health / University of Aberdeen addresses a critical gap: how to properly evaluate automatically generated clinical consultation notes. The study deployed 5 UK GPs to evaluate 285 notes (57 consultations × 5 notes each) across 4 tasks: listen/take notes, post-edit, classify errors, and qualitative feedback. Then correlated human judgments with 18 automatic quality metrics. Key findings: (1) **Omissions** are the most common and clinically significant error type — consistent with Biro 2025, Anderson 2025, Arko 2025, found independently in this earlier study; (2) **Levenshtein distance** (simple character-based edit distance) performs as well as or better than model-based metrics like BertScore for this task; (3) ROUGE and other automatic metrics correlate poorly with human clinical judgment; (4) Post-editing generated notes can be faster than writing from scratch but may be more cognitively intensive. All annotations open-sourced (PriMock57 dataset). This paper predates modern commercial ambient AI scribes but establishes the methodological foundation for how to evaluate them.
hypothesis: Explicit: to present a human evaluation study of consultation notes and establish which automatic metrics best correlate with human clinical judgment.

setting: Moffitt Cancer Center (NCI-designated Comprehensive Cancer Center in southeastern US). 21 clinician champions participated in pilot. 10 completed baseline survey (47.6% response); 9 completed 1-month survey; 8 completed interview. Cancer care clinicians (physicians and APPs).
evidenceLevel: Mixed-methods longitudinal pilot study (pre/post surveys + semistructured interviews)
dataSources: Surveys (demographics, Mini-Z burnout, sleep quality, implementation outcomes) | Semistructured interviews (CFIR framework) | Complete case analysis for missing data
summary: This mixed-methods pilot at Moffitt Cancer Center assessed a digital scribe (DS) among cancer care clinicians. DS rated as marginally acceptable (16.0), appropriate (16.3), and usable (68.6). Feasibility slightly below cutoff (15.2 vs 16.0). No significant improvement in burnout (Mini-Z 33.3 vs 35.9, p=.257), but improved perceptions of having sufficient documentation time (2.1 vs 3.6, p=.005). Implementation facilitators: individualized training, on-site vendor support. Barriers: workflow integration challenges, editing workload, patient privacy concerns with smartphone recording. Clinicians initially used DS with straightforward patients; some expanded to all visits, others limited to specific cases. Editing workload improved over time. Most edits needed on Assessment and Plan sections. Telemedicine integration reported as feasible by those who used it.
hypothesis: Explicit: to evaluate DS feasibility, acceptability, appropriateness, usability, and preliminary association with clinician well-being in cancer care setting.

title: Use of Ambient AI Scribes to Reduce Administrative Burden and Professional Burnout
authors: Olson, Kristine D.; Meeker, Daniella; Troup, Matt; Barker, Timothy D.; Nguyen, Vinh H.; Manders, Jennifer B.; Stults, Cheryl D.; Jones, Veena G.; Shah, Sachin D.; Shah, Tina; Schwamm, Lee H.; et al.

summary: This large multi-site pre/post study of 263 clinicians across 6 health systems evaluated the impact of Abridge ambient AI scribes on burnout and administrative burden. Primary outcome: self-reported burnout (10-point scale). Burnout improved significantly: mean 4.59 → 4.12 (−0.47, p<.001); multivariable model: OR 0.26 [0.13, 0.54], p<.001 — i.e., 74% reduction in odds of burnout. This is one of the largest and most diverse samples in the corpus (n=263, 6 sites, full specialty mix including surgery). The inclusion of an Abridge employee as a co-author is a COI to note. id: Omon 2025 - Generative AI Rehabilitation Clinical Documentation
title: Effects of Introducing Generative AI in Rehabilitation Clinical Documentation
authors: Omon, Kyohei; Sasaki, Tomohiko; Koshiro, Ryota; Fuchigami, Takeshi; Hamashima, Masahiro
journal: Cureus | Vol. 17, No. 3 | March 27, 2025 | e81313. Open Access (CC-BY 4.0). Review period: March 21–25, 2025.
product: **medimo** (developed by Pleap Inc., Tokyo, Japan — https://pleap.jp/). Web application that transcribes voice inputs from healthcare professionals during patient consultations and summarizes content using AI. For this study, medimo was enhanced by Pleap Inc. with a newly collected speech dataset specifically designed for rehabilitation professionals. **Not conversational ambient listening** — uses non-conversational voice memos recorded by clinicians, not live encounter capture.
setting: Saito Rehabilitation Hospital (Osaka) and Kawanishi Rehabilitation Hospital (Hyogo), Japan. Participants: 12 rehabilitation professionals — 2 physical therapists, 2 occupational therapists, 2 speech-language-hearing therapists (from each of 2 hospitals). All had ≥6 months at institution and ≥5 years clinical experience. Mean age 33.2 ± 6.8 years; mean experience 10.1 ± 3.1 years; 4 male, 8 female. Prospective comparative AB design: Phase A = 5 days conventional documentation; Phase B = 5 days using medimo. IRB approved.
evidenceLevel: Observational / QI (prospective comparative AB design — within-subjects, pre/post)
dataSources: Direct time measurement (third-party observer) | Validated survey (NASA-TLX) | Blinded quality scoring (3 independent physical therapists, custom 6-item 10-point scale)
summary: This small Japanese study (n=12) compared conventional clinical documentation (Phase A) vs. AI-assisted documentation using "medimo" (Phase B) among rehabilitation professionals across two hospitals. The AB design used direct third-party time measurement, NASA-TLX workload assessment, and blinded quality scoring. Key findings: (1) Documentation time was NOT significantly reduced (305 ± 53.9 s in Phase A vs. 336 ± 78.0 s in Phase B; ns) — attributable to the editing burden when voice input was insufficient; (2) NASA-TLX **Temporal Demand (time pressure)** was significantly lower with medimo (p=0.037) even without time savings — suggesting reduction in perceived urgency even if total time didn't drop; (3) NASA-TLX **Frustration (dissatisfaction)** was significantly HIGHER with medimo (p=0.041) — attributed to low AI literacy and skill gaps with a novel workflow; (4) Documentation quality (SOAP sections S and P, total score) was significantly higher with medimo (p=0.045, 0.045, 0.023 respectively); (5) Strong positive correlation between number of voice memos recorded and documentation time reduction (r=0.71, p=0.006) — suggesting that tool utilization intensity drives benefit; (6) Significant positive correlation between time reduction and implementation intent/willingness to adopt (r=0.67, p=0.009). Authors conclude that user motivation and AI literacy are key moderators of benefit.
hypothesis: Explicit: to evaluate whether the use of generative AI reduces clinical documentation time, alleviates workload, and improves quality in a rehabilitation setting.

measures.description: Third-party-measured time from sitting at EMR to completed registration. Phase A: 305 ± 53.9 s; Phase B: 336 ± 78.0 s. **No significant difference** (p=ns). Editing burden from incomplete voice input offset any AI efficiency gain.

NASA-TLX subscale on perceived time urgency during documentation (0–100 VAS). Significantly lower with medimo (AVE Phase A: 58.0, Phase B: 49.8; p=0.037). Only subscale showing significant improvement.
