Mental demand subscale. No significant difference (53.3 → 52.0).
Physical demand subscale. No significant difference (42.9 → 43.9).
Self-rated task performance subscale. No significant difference (41.5 → 47.3).
Effort subscale. No significant difference (56.1 → 53.7).

Blinded score (3 independent PTs, 10-point scale) for the Subjective section of SOAP notes. Significantly higher with medimo (AVE 8.3 → 8.4; p=0.045). Real-time recording captured patient complaints more accurately.
Blinded score for the Plan section of SOAP notes. Significantly higher with medimo (AVE 5.4 → 6.7; p=0.045). Largest quality gain; real-time capture improved treatment plan specificity.

Sum of all 6 quality subscale scores (Details, Non-use of technical jargon, Appropriate expressions, SOAP_S, SOAP_O, SOAP_P). Significantly higher with medimo (AVE 40.9 → 44.9; p=0.023). SOAP_O (Objective) did not reach significance alone.

Count of non-conversational voice memos recorded per participant during Phase B. Strong positive correlation with documentation time reduction (r=0.71, p=0.006). Behavioral proxy for tool engagement and utilization intensity.

Post-study 5-point scale: "If medimo were introduced, would you use it?" Significant positive correlation with time reduction (r=0.67, p=0.009). User motivation is a key predictor of achieving time benefits.

id: Owens 2024 - Ambient Voice Technology Patient Physician Relationship
title: Effect of Ambient Voice Technology, Natural Language Processing, and Artificial Intelligence on the Patient–Physician Relationship
authors: Owens, Lance M.; Wilda, J. Joshua; Grifka, Ronald G.; Westendorp, Joan; Fletcher, Jeffrey J.
journal: Applied Clinical Informatics | Vol. 15, No. 4 | 2024 | pp. 660–667. Thieme. University of Michigan Health-West, Wyoming Michigan. Received March 15, 2024; Accepted May 31, 2024; Published August 7, 2024.
product: **DAX Copilot** (Nuance/Microsoft). Primary care ambulatory settings at University of Michigan Health-West.
setting: University of Michigan Health-West (community-based academic health system, Wyoming Michigan). Prospective observational study with two phases: (1) **Open-label phase** — 288 patients whose physicians used DAX; patients knew DAX was being used; (2) **Masked phase** — 304 patients compared DAX encounters vs. non-DAX encounters, blinded to whether DAX was used. Primary outcome: Patient-Doctor Relationship Questionnaire-9 (PDRQ-9) score. STROBE guidelines followed.
evidenceLevel: Observational (prospective, two-arm: open-label + masked comparison)
dataSources: Patient survey (PDRQ-9 — validated 9-item patient-reported outcome measuring patient-physician relationship) | Open-label patient satisfaction questions
summary: This is the only study in the corpus using a **validated patient-reported outcome instrument** (PDRQ-9) to measure the impact of ambient AI scribes on the patient-physician relationship. Two-phase design: open-label (n=288, patients knew DAX was used) and masked (n=304, patients blinded to DAX use). Key findings: In the **open-label phase**, patients strongly agreed that physicians were more focused on them, spent less time typing, and made encounters feel more personable — positive open-label signals. However, in the **masked phase**, NO difference was detected in PDRQ-9 scores between DAX and non-DAX encounters (median 45 vs. 45; p=0.31). Adjusted OR for complete satisfaction on "how well clinician listened" was 0.8 (95% CI 0.48–1.34) — not significant. Conclusion: patients strongly endorse DAX when they know it's being used (social desirability/expectation effect), but when blinded, no measurable difference in the patient-physician relationship on a validated scale. This is a critical methodological finding for the field.
hypothesis: Explicit: to evaluate how use of ambient voice recognition with NLP and AI (DAX) affects the patient-physician relationship.

measures.description: 9-item validated Patient-Doctor Relationship Questionnaire. No difference detected: DAX median 45 (IQR 8) vs. no-DAX median 45 (IQR 3.5); p=0.31. aOR for complete satisfaction on "how well clinician listened" = 0.8 [0.48–1.34], NS.
Open-label: patients strongly agreed physician was more focused on them when DAX was used.

id: Palm 2025 - Quality AI Generated Clinical Notes PDQI Validation
title: Assessing the quality of AI-generated clinical notes: validated evaluation of a large language model ambient scribe
authors: Palm, Erin; Manikantan, Astrit; Mahal, Herprit; Belwadi Subramanya, Srikanth; Pepin, Mark E.
journal: Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence | Section: Medicine and Public Health | Vol. 8 | October 21, 2025. Open Access.
product: **Suki** — explicitly named. "Suki is an ambient clinical documentation system that summarizes audio-recorded medical interactions into structured clinical notes." Both proprietary and fine-tuned third-party LLMs are used. All authors are Suki AI employees (Redwood City, CA) or affiliated with Stanford/Santa Clara Valley Medical Center. ⚠️ **Industry-authored study** — Suki employees evaluated Suki's own product.
setting: 97 de-identified audio recordings of outpatient clinical encounters across 5 specialties: general medicine (20), pediatrics (20), OB/gyn (17), orthopedics (20), adult cardiology (20). Audio pulled from Suki's production database (October 2024). All encounters anonymized by Suki operations team. No clinical sites named. Two blinded specialty reviewers evaluated each encounter's AI ("Ambient") note vs. physician-authored ("Gold") note.
evidenceLevel: Observational (cross-sectional note quality evaluation — prospective generation, blinded comparative review)
dataSources: AI-generated notes (Suki Ambient) | Physician-authored reference notes (Gold notes, written from same audio/transcript) | Blinded expert review using modified PDQI-9 (11 criteria, 1–5 Likert + binary hallucination detection)
summary: This industry study from Suki AI compared the quality of Suki-generated "Ambient" clinical notes to blinded physician-authored "Gold" notes across 97 encounters in 5 specialties, using a modified PDQI-9 instrument (8 original PDQI-9 items minus "Up to date" + "Appropriate for specialty" + "Fair" + binary "Hallucination"). 388 total note evaluations. Key results from Table 4: Gold notes scored modestly higher overall (4.25 vs. 4.20, p=0.04). Gold notes were superior on Accurate (p=0.05), Succinct (p<0.001), and Internally Consistent (p=0.004). Ambient notes were superior on Thorough (p<0.001) and Organized (p=0.03). Hallucinations: present in 31% of Ambient notes vs. 20% of Gold notes (p=0.01). Despite these deficits, reviewers overall *preferred* Ambient notes 47% vs. 39% for Gold (14% equal preference). Interrater agreement was high overall (RWG>0.7) except cardiology (moderate agreement for 6/11 criteria). OB/Gyn and pediatrics tended to favor Gold; general medicine, orthopedics, and cardiology tended to favor Ambient.
hypothesis: Explicit: to compare quality of LLM-generated Ambient notes with physician-authored reference notes across five clinical specialties using PDQI-9 as validation framework.

id: Pearlman 2025 - AI Scribe EHR Efficiency
title: Use of an AI Scribe and Electronic Health Record Efficiency
authors: Pearlman, Kevin; Wan, Wen; Shah, Sachin; Laiteerapong, Neda; et al.
journal: JAMA Network Open | 2025. Open access. University of Chicago, Department of Medicine.
product: AI scribe (unnamed in available text; study context is University of Chicago ambulatory pilot, July–September 2024). Likely Abridge or DAX Copilot based on institutional context. Product identified as "AI scribe pilot."
setting: Large academic health system (University of Chicago Medicine). Pilot group: n=125 clinicians who opted into AI scribe pilot; Control group: n=478 matched clinicians who did not. Baseline period: April–June 2024; Intervention period: July–September 2024. Propensity score weighting used to balance groups. Mix of primary care (27%), medical subspecialty (37%), and surgical/procedural (36%). Physicians (93.6%) and advanced practice professionals (6.4%).
evidenceLevel: Observational (quasi-experimental, propensity score-weighted comparison with concurrent control group)
dataSources: EHR data (Epic EHR telemetry — time in EHR, time in notes, after-hours time, encounter closure time)
summary: This methodologically rigorous study at University of Chicago used propensity score weighting to compare EHR efficiency outcomes between 125 AI scribe pilot clinicians and 478 matched controls over a 3-month intervention period. Primary outcomes were objective EHR telemetry metrics. Key findings: AI scribe use was associated with significant reductions in total EHR time per appointment (−2.0 min, p<.001) and time in notes per appointment (−0.5 min, p<.001) compared to control. Notably, after-hours documentation time did NOT change significantly (−0.5 min, p=.95) — a consistent null finding across both groups. Benefit was strongly dose-dependent: clinicians in the highest AI scribe use quartile (Q4) showed the largest reductions; low users (Q1) showed minimal or no benefit. Generalized linear models adjusted for sex, years since training, specialty, clinician type, hospital-based vs. offsite clinic, Epic proficiency score, and baseline values. Authors note this is among the most methodologically rigorous studies in the field due to the concurrent control group and propensity score balancing.
hypothesis: Explicit: to evaluate whether AI scribe use is associated with reduced EHR time and improved efficiency metrics.

PHTI is an independent nonprofit funded by the Peterson Center on Healthcare; it provides evidence-based evaluations with no vendor funding. The report's AI Taskforce included health system leaders and AI solution companies convened September 2024–early 2025. id: Prasad 2025 - Clinician Perceptions Virtual Scribe Use Survey
title: Clinician Perceptions of Virtual Scribe Use: A Survey Study
authors: Prasad, Kriti; Frits, Michelle; Iannaccone, Christine; Melnick, Edward R.; Zhang, Jianyi; Mugal, Aqsa; Lipsitz, Stuart; Healey, Michael; Holland, Christopher; Snyder, Richard; Sinsky, Christine; Ting, David; Bates, David W.; Rotenstein, Lisa
journal: Journal of General Internal Medicine | Published online August 5, 2025. Springer/Society of General Internal Medicine.
product: **Virtual scribes — mix of synchronous and asynchronous human/human-assisted remote scribing services** at Mass General Brigham (Brigham and Women's Hospital), Boston MA. Services used include:
setting: Mass General Brigham (Brigham and Women's Hospital, Massachusetts General Hospital), Boston MA. Large academic health system. Virtual scribe program running since 2017. Survey conducted November 2022–January 2023. 450 eligible participants; 20 excluded (never used or <1 week); 430 eligible → 120 responded → 7 NPs excluded → **113 physicians analyzed** (26.3% response rate). Ambulatory physicians only. 68.1% female; 77.9% primary care; 60.2% used ≥2 years.
evidenceLevel: Observational (cross-sectional mixed-methods — quantitative survey + deductive thematic qualitative analysis)
dataSources: Survey (14-item REDCap survey) | Qualitative (free-text survey comments, deductive thematic analysis)
summary: Cross-sectional mixed-methods survey of 113 ambulatory physicians at Mass General Brigham who used virtual scribe services from 2017–2023. Overall results were mixed: 47.8% were satisfied, 66.4% reported improved wellbeing, 61.1% reported enhanced patient relationships. The most frequently cited benefits were better focus on patients during visits (50%) and reduced documentation time (53%); the most common drawback was a steep learning curve. Multivariable analyses revealed strong moderators: non-primary care physicians were substantially more likely to benefit on all three outcomes (wellbeing aOR 34.60 [5.26, 227.64]; satisfaction aOR 10.12 [2.06, 49.62]; patient relationship aOR 6.34 [1.24, 32.34]). Males were more likely than females to report improved satisfaction and patient relationships. Paradoxically, shorter scribe tenure (<2 years) was associated with dramatically reduced odds of satisfaction and patient relationship benefit — suggesting benefits accrue with adaptation time. Qualitative themes highlighted service reliability concerns (scribe turnover, training burden, variable quality), documentation problems (poor quality, paradoxical documentation time increases, errors), and workflow impacts (delayed encounter closure alongside positive impacts like more time for patients). Authors conclude specialty and gender are key moderators and call for further investigation into primary care vs. non-primary care differential.
hypothesis: Explicit: to characterize the relationship between virtual scribe use and physician wellbeing and identify physician characteristics associated with benefitting from virtual scribe support.

"Somewhat/strongly agree" that virtual scribe enhances wellbeing. 66.4% endorsed. Largest effect for non-primary care: aOR 34.60 [5.26, 227.64]. Not significantly associated with gender for wellbeing.

id: Shah 2024 - Ambient AI Scribes Physician Burnout Usability Documentation Burden
title: Ambient artificial intelligence scribes: physician burnout and perspectives on usability and documentation burden
authors: Shah, Shreya J.; Devon-Sand, Anna; Ma, Stephen P.; Smith, Margaret; Jeong, Yejin; Crowell, Trevor; Delahaie, Clarissa; Hsia, Caroline; Lin, Steven; Shanafelt, Tait; Pfeffer, Michael A.; Sharp, Christopher; Garcia, Patricia
journal: Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association (JAMIA) | Vol. 32, Issue 2 | Pages 375–380 | 2025 (published December 2024, PMID: 39657021; PMC: PMC11756571)
product: **Nuance DAX Copilot** — integrated with Epic EHR (Epic Haiku mobile app) at Stanford Health Care. The tool generated draft notes in 4 SmartSections: HPI, physical exam, results, and assessment and plan. Attestation SmartSection also included.
setting: Outpatient ambulatory — Stanford Health Care (academic medical center), both faculty and community practice networks. Primary care (66%) and ambulatory specialties (34%): cardiology, gastroenterology, rheumatology, neurology, ophthalmology, otolaryngology, geriatrics. October 2023–January 2024 (3-month pilot, 48 physicians).
evidenceLevel: Quality Improvement (prospective)
dataSources: Survey/self-report (pre/post paired survey); product telemetry (utilization data)
summary: One of the earliest published QI studies of a large-scale institutional ambient AI scribe implementation. 48 physicians at Stanford Health Care used DAX Copilot for 3 months. id: Shuaib 2021 - Scribe Program Patient Throughput Physician Productivity
title: Impact of a scribe program on patient throughput, physician productivity, and patient satisfaction in a community-based emergency department
authors: Shuaib, Waqas; Hilmi, John; Caballero, Joshua; Rashid, Ijaz; Stanazai, Hashim; Ajanovic, Alan; Moshtaghi, Alex; Amari, Ahmed; Tawfeek, Kerolos; Khurana, Anjit; Hasabo, Hesham; Baqais, Abdulrehman; Mattar, Ahmed A.; Gaeta, Theodore J.
journal: Health Informatics Journal | Vol. 27, No. 1 | 2021. Published 2018 online (received 2017). SAGE Publications.
product: **Human medical scribes** (not AI/ambient technology). Scribe program implemented by a private-sector scribe company; scribes are college students/recent graduates trained on-the-job (proficient after 20 shifts, skilled after 40 shifts). One scribe assigned per physician. All scribe-generated charts forwarded to physician for review, amendment, and signature. ⚠️ This paper is a **human scribe baseline** — not an ambient AI study. Included in the corpus as a pre-AI comparator.
setting: Suburban non-academic community emergency department, ~80,000 visits/year, Level II Trauma Center (American College of Surgeons), treating adults and pediatrics. Study period: July 2015–February 2016. Pre-implementation baseline: 90 days (July 1–Sept 30, 2015); 2-month washout/phase-in; post-implementation: 90 days (Dec 1, 2015–Feb 28, 2016). Physicians only (NPs/PAs excluded). 3–4 scribes available at all times. No staff changes during study.
evidenceLevel: Observational (uncontrolled before-and-after / quasi-experimental)
dataSources: EHR data (quality assurance reports from EHR system) | Survey (Press Ganey patient satisfaction) | Time-motion analysis (direct observation)
summary: This prospective before-and-after study at a community ED (n=12,721 pre-scribe, n=13,598 post-scribe patient encounters) evaluated the impact of a human medical scribe program on throughput, physician productivity, and patient satisfaction. All five throughput metrics improved significantly (all p<0.0001): door-to-doc time improved from 61 to 56 min; LOS for discharged patients improved from 303 to 287 min. Physician productivity metrics improved substantially: patients per hour increased from 2.3 to 3.2 (p<0.001), total wRVUs per hour from 3.1 to 5.2 (p<0.001). Time-motion analysis showed post-visit documentation time halved (14→7 min, p<0.01), while doctor-patient interaction time nearly doubled (4→7.8 min, p<0.01). Patient satisfaction was already high and remained high; the only statistically significant satisfaction item was "clinician providing satisfactory feedback" (3.9→4.7, p<0.01). Physician ED satisfaction percentile improved from 66% to 81% (15-point gain). Authors conclude human scribes are feasible and effective in community EDs (most prior evidence was from academic institutions).
hypothesis: Explicit: to evaluate the impact of medical scribes on patient throughput, physician productivity, and patient satisfaction in a community ED setting.

id: Stults 2025 - Evaluation Ambient AI Documentation Platform Clinicians
title: Evaluation of an Ambient Artificial Intelligence Documentation Platform for Clinicians
authors: Stults, Cheryl D.; Deng, Sien; Martinez, Meghan C.; Wilcox, Joseph; Szwerinski, Nina; Chen, Kevin H.; Driscoll, Stephanie; Washburn, Joanna; Jones, Veena G.
journal: JAMA Network Open | 2025
product: Abridge (identified from qualitative quotes in Table 3 — e.g., "I have loved my experience with Abridge thus far"). Paper does not explicitly name the product in the methods, but quotes make identification clear. Integrated with Epic at Sutter Health.
setting: Sutter Health, Palo Alto, California. 100 clinicians enrolled; 57 completed both pre- and post-implementation surveys. Mixed specialties: primary care (58%), medical subspecialty (21%), surgical subspecialty (21%). Physicians (92%) and advanced practice clinicians (8%). Pre/post survey design combined with EHR Signal data.
evidenceLevel: Observational (pre/post mixed-methods: quantitative survey + EHR telemetry)
dataSources: Survey/self-report | EHR data (Epic Signal) | Mixed
summary: This quality improvement study at Sutter Health (Northern and Central California) evaluated an ambient AI documentation platform (identified as Abridge in qualitative quotes) across 100 clinicians, with 57 completing both pre- and post-implementation surveys. Ambient AI was associated with significantly decreased time in notes per appointment (mean 6.2 → 5.3 min, p<.001), with a notable sex difference: women saw a larger reduction (8.1 → 6.7 min) than men (4.7 → 4.2 min; DiD p=.001). Documentation and progress note lengths increased slightly. NASA-TLX subscores (mental demand, temporal demand, effort) all improved significantly (all p<.001). However, Mini-Z burnout scores did not change significantly (42.1% pre vs. 35.1% post, p=.12). Clinicians reporting ≤1 hr of after-hours note writing increased from 14% to 54.4% (p<.001). Qualitative themes: improved note comprehensiveness, more patient face time, reduced cognitive load, and joy of medicine restored — balanced against EHR integration friction, inability to customize note style, and specialty-specific limitations. Off-hours EHR activity did not change significantly (p=.14 overall).
hypothesis: Not explicitly stated. Implicit hypothesis: ambient AI documentation will improve clinician experience of documentation burden, burnout, and EHR efficiency.

id: Suhail 2026 - Metrics for Ambient Clinical Documentation Scoping Review
title: Current evidence and future directions of metrics used to evaluate ambient clinical documentation: A scoping review
authors: Suhail, Dernas; Wong, Zhen Yu; Ubhi, Jeevan; Kungwengwe, Garikai; Faderani, Ryan; Mosahebi, Afshin
journal: International Journal of Medical Informatics | Vol. 205 | January 2026 | Article 106113. Received 25 July 2025; Accepted 9 September 2025; Available online 10 September 2025.
product: N/A — scoping review of 11 primary studies. Products in included studies: Nuance Dragon Ambient Experience (DAX) used in 3 of 11 studies; Dragon Medical 11.0 in 2; other systems (ChatGPT-4, AWS Transcribe, Google Speech-to-Text, proprietary) in remainder.
setting: Scoping review — synthesizes 11 primary studies. Settings across included studies: USA (7), Australia (2), Germany (1). Mix of simulated consultations, inpatient, and outpatient settings. Medline and Embase search (4,949 articles identified → 11 included via PRISMA-ScR).
evidenceLevel: Scoping Review (PRISMA-ScR compliant)
dataSources: Synthesis of published primary literature (Medline + Embase)
summary: This peer-reviewed scoping review — the direct counterpart to the Gebauer 2025 preprint (which it explicitly cites and positions itself as the peer-reviewed alternative to) — synthesizes evaluation metrics from 11 primary studies on ambient clinical documentation (ACD). The 11 included studies span 6 comparative experimental studies, 2 mixed-methods observational studies, 1 cross-sectional study, 1 prospective experimental study, and 1 peer-matched cohort study. Four main evaluation themes were identified: (1) **model performance** (7/11 studies — F1, ROUGE, BERTScore, BLEURT, PDQI, custom entity-linking metrics), (2) **documentation efficiency** (3/11 — time per patient, out-of-hours documentation, words per minute, WRVU), (3) **clinician experience** (3/11 — OLBI burnout, Press Ganey Engagement, Mini-Z Survey, PROMIS sleep, FIM/AIM/IAM/IUS implementation measures), and (4) **patient experience** (1/11 — patient likelihood to recommend, opt-out rate, safety events). Significant heterogeneity in metrics across studies was the central finding. The review proposes the **Ambient Clinical Documentation Quality Instrument (ACDQI)** as a standardized scoring framework. Notably, the ACDQI as proposed is limited to model performance and documentation efficiency — clinician and patient experience domains are explicitly excluded from the scored instrument due to susceptibility to confounding, though their importance is acknowledged.
hypothesis: Not explicitly stated. Implicit objective: to synthesize the landscape of ACD evaluation metrics and propose a standardized framework (ACDQI) for future studies.

id: Tierney 2024 - Ambient Artificial Intelligence Scribes Alleviate Burden Clinical Documentation
title: Ambient Artificial Intelligence Scribes to Alleviate the Burden of Clinical Documentation
authors: Tierney, Aaron A.; Gayre, Gregg; Hoberman, Brian; Mattern, Britt; Ballesca, Manuel; Kipnis, Patricia; Liu, Vincent; Lee, Kristine
journal: NEJM Catalyst Innovations in Care Delivery | Vol. 5, No. 3 | February 21, 2024. The Permanente Medical Group (TPMG), Kaiser Permanente Northern California. Large-scale implementation study.
product: **Ambient AI scribe technology** (Nuance DAX Copilot). Smartphone microphone to transcribe encounters; does not retain audio recordings. EHR-integrated. Pilot enabled for 10,000 physicians and staff across diverse settings and specialties at TPMG in October 2023.
setting: The Permanente Medical Group (TPMG), Kaiser Permanente Northern California. Large-scale implementation across diverse settings and specialties. In 10 weeks since implementation, used by 3,442 physicians in as many as 303,266 patient encounters. 968 physicians enabled ambient AI scribes in ≥100 patient encounters; one physician in 1,210 encounters. Early evaluation metrics based on existing tool that evaluates quality of human-generated scribe notes.
evidenceLevel: Observational (large-scale implementation study with early evaluation metrics)
dataSources: Utilization data (303,266 encounters) | Early evaluation metrics based on human-generated scribe note quality tool | Statistical analyses of time spent in documentation and EHR
summary: This large-scale implementation study at TPMG enabled ambient AI scribe technology for 10,000 physicians and staff in October 2023. In 10 weeks, used by 3,442 physicians in 303,266 patient encounters across diverse specialties. Response from physicians who used the tool has been favorable — they cite the technology’s capability to facilitate more personal, meaningful, and effective patient interactions and to reduce the burden of after-hours clerical work. Early assessments of patient feedback have been positive, with some describing improved interaction with their physicians. Early evaluation metrics, based on an existing tool that evaluates the quality of human-generated scribe notes, find that ambient AI use produces high-quality clinical documentation for physicians’ editing. Further statistical analyses after AI scribe implementation also find that usage is linked with reduced time spent in documentation and in the EHR. Ongoing enhancements focused on direct EHR integration, improved capabilities for incorporating medical interpretation, and enhanced workflow personalization options for individual users. Despite early promise, careful attention must be paid to ensure the technology supports clinicians while optimizing output for accuracy, relevance, and alignment in the physician–patient relationship.
hypothesis: Explicit: ambient AI scribes have great potential to reduce documentation burden, enhance physician–patient encounters, and augment clinicians’ capabilities.

id: Tierney 2025 - Ambient AI Scribes Learnings 1 Year 2.5 Million Uses
title: Ambient Artificial Intelligence Scribes: Learnings after 1 Year and over 2.5 Million Uses
authors: Tierney, Aaron A.; Gayre, Gregg; Hoberman, Brian; Mattern, Britt; Ballesca, Manuel; Wilson Hannay, Sarah B.; Castilla, Kate; Lau, Cindy S.; Kipnis, Patricia; Liu, Vincent; Lee, Kristine
journal: NEJM Catalyst Innovations in Care Delivery | Vol. 6, No. 5 | May 2025
product: Two unnamed ambient AI scribe vendors — The Permanente Medical Group (Kaiser Permanente) transitioned between vendors during the study period. Both vendors unnamed throughout. This is the 1-year follow-up to Tierney 2024 (initial pilot, also in corpus).
setting: Adult and Family Medicine (primary survey cohort); plus mental health, emergency medicine, and multiple specialties across enterprise. The Permanente Medical Group (TPMG), Kaiser Permanente — large integrated health system, all 8 KP regions.
evidenceLevel: Quality Improvement / Operational Longitudinal Report (Commentary format). Within-physician difference-in-differences analysis for workload metrics. Survey for physician and patient experience.

summary: One-year operational follow-up of ambient AI scribe deployment at TPMG/Kaiser Permanente, building on Tierney 2024 (10-week pilot). Covers Oct 16, 2023 – Dec 28, 2024 (63 weeks). 7,260 physicians used the tool across 2,576,627 patient encounters. Usage grew linearly; top-third high users drove 89% of total activations. Dose-response confirmed: high users saved 0.7 min/note vs. 0.15 min/note for low users. Aggregate savings: 15,791 hours (~1,794 8-hour working days) vs. nonusers. DiD analysis shows significant reductions in pajama time, after-hours work, notes time, and orders time; small increase in in-basket time. Physician survey: 84% positive impact on visit interactions, 82% positive work satisfaction. Patient survey: 56% positive impact on visit quality, 0% negative, 92% comfortable or neutral with AI. Organization switched vendors mid-period; adoption remained stable, with prior usage patterns predicting subsequent usage. No COI — entirely industry-independent TPMG report.
hypothesis: Not formally stated (commentary format). Key questions investigated: (1) Is AI scribe use growing/sustainable? (2) Does dose-response exist? (3) What physician characteristics predict adoption? (4) What is the physician and patient experience? (5) What happens when you switch vendors?

Prior usage predicted future usage: 78% of high users stayed high; 50% of low users stayed low; 17% of low users became high with new vendor
Behavior is largely vendor-agnostic — habits transfer across platforms
Single large integrated health system (results may differ elsewhere)
Vendor transition prevented direct matching of usage data across full period
Could not evaluate note editing proportion over full period (only available early)
Physician and patient surveys not linked to demographic data — cannot analyze differential experience by sociodemographic group
Future work needed: qualitative analyses, health equity dimensions, patients with disabilities (speech impairment), trust

id: Wang 2021 - Patient-Centered Digital Scribe Automatic Medical Documentation
title: A patient-centered digital scribe for automatic medical documentation
authors: Wang, Jesse; Lavender, Marc; Hoque, Ehsan; Brophy, Patrick; Kautz, Henry
journal: JAMIA Open | Vol. 4, No. 1 | 2021 | ooab003. Open Access. University of Rochester Medical Center. Proof-of-concept study with two medical students.
product: **Patient-centered digital scribe**. Uses summarizing and signposting from patient-centered communication to document history sections. Uses text classification to simultaneously document physical exam. Not a commercial product — custom system developed for this study. Audio captured with earset microphone; transcribed with Google Speech-to-Text video model; diarized and punctuated.
setting: Simulation study with two medical students alternating as physician and patient. 57 mock consultations from PriMock57 dataset (Babylon Health). Consultations ~10 minutes long, covering common primary care topics. 5 regularly practicing UK GPs (≥3 years experience) evaluated notes over 8 weeks (~30 working hours/evaluator). Lead Clinician (author) supervised evaluation. All clinicians paid UK standard GP working rate.
evidenceLevel: Observational (simulation study — human evaluation + automatic metric correlation)
dataSources: Human evaluation (5 GPs): listen/take notes, post-edit 5 notes per consultation, classify errors, qualitative feedback | 18 automatic NLP metrics (ROUGE, BertScore, Levenshtein, etc.) | Post-editing time measurement
summary: This foundational NLP methodology paper addresses how to properly evaluate automatically generated clinical consultation notes. The study deployed 5 UK GPs to evaluate 285 notes (57 consultations × 5 notes each) across 4 tasks: listen and take notes, post-edit, classify errors (incorrect statements, omissions), and provide qualitative feedback. Then correlated human judgments with 18 automatic quality metrics. Key findings: (1) Omissions are the most common and clinically significant error type — consistent with later simulation studies (Biro 2025, Anderson 2025, Arko 2025); (2) Simple Levenshtein distance (character-based edit distance) performs as well as or better than model-based metrics like BertScore for this task; (3) ROUGE and other automatic metrics correlate poorly with human clinical judgment; (4) Post-editing generated notes can be faster than writing from scratch but may be more cognitively intensive. All annotations open-sourced (PriMock57 dataset). This paper predates modern commercial ambient AI scribes but establishes the methodological foundation for how to evaluate them. Dataset released as open source.
hypothesis: Explicit: to present a human evaluation study of consultation notes and establish which automatic metrics best correlate with human clinical judgment.

measures.description: Time to correct AI-generated note to factual accuracy. Key efficiency metric for real-world scribe use. Prior work showed post-editing faster but more cognitively intensive than writing from scratch.
measures.dimensions: II - Individual Impact, US - User Satisfaction

measures.description: Factual errors in generated notes vs. consultation audio. Sentences containing one or more factual inaccuracies.
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, SQ - System Quality

measures.description: Medical facts that should be in note but omitted by model. Most common error type. Critical omissions flagged for medico-legal liability.
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, SQ - System Quality

measures.description: Errors with essential clinical importance — would lead to medico-legal liability. Safety-weighted error classification.
18 metrics evaluated (ROUGE variants, BertScore, Levenshtein, others). Key finding: Levenshtein distance correlates as well as or better than BertScore with human judgments. ROUGE correlates poorly.
measures.dimensions: SQ - System Quality, IQ - Information Quality

measures.description: Issues with order of statements, repetition, and other structural concerns beyond factual errors.

id: Wang 2025 - Evaluation Framework Ambient Digital Scribing Tools
title: An evaluation framework for ambient digital scribing tools in clinical applications
authors: Wang, Haoyuan; Yang, Rui; Alwakeel, Mahmoud; Kayastha, Ankit; Chowdhury, Anand; Biro, Joshua M.; Sorrentino, Anthony D.; Handley, Jessica L.; Hantzmon, Sarah; Bessias, Sophia; Economou-Zavlanos, Nicoleta J.; Bedoya, Armando; Agrawal, Monica; Ratwani, Raj M.; Poon, Eric G.; Pencina, Michael J.; Pollak, Kathryn I.; Hong, Chuan
journal: npj Digital Medicine | 2025
product: An internally developed ADS tool (built by the authors) — used as a test case to validate the proposed framework. Also references DAX Copilot and Abridge in the literature context, but does not directly evaluate those products.
setting: 40 real clinical visit recordings used for validation. Academic/clinical research setting. Not a specific health system deployment — framework validation study.
evidenceLevel: Observational (framework development + empirical validation on 40 real recordings)
dataSources: Mixed (automated NLP metrics + human expert evaluation + LLM-as-evaluator + simulation testing)
summary: This paper proposes a comprehensive evaluation framework for ambient digital scribing (ADS) tools, spanning four evaluation modalities: human evaluation, automated metrics, simulation testing, and LLMs-as-evaluators. The framework assesses three pipeline stages: transcription, speaker diarization, and medical note generation. Evaluation criteria include fluency, completeness, and factuality. Applied to 40 real clinical recordings, the framework identified strengths (fluency, clarity) and weaknesses (factual accuracy, new medication capture). The paper is one of the most technically rigorous framework proposals in the corpus and directly relevant to the Regenstrief/Suki evaluation design work.
hypothesis: Not explicitly stated as a testable hypothesis. The paper aims to demonstrate that the proposed multi-modal evaluation framework can identify both strengths and weaknesses in ADS tool performance more comprehensively than existing single-method approaches.

measures.description: Standard metric measuring how accurately the system converts spoken audio to text. Baseline quality indicator for the upstream pipeline stage.
Ability of the system to correctly attribute spoken segments to the correct speaker (clinician vs. patient). Errors here propagate to note quality.
Human-rated measure of how naturally and coherently the generated clinical note reads — assessing readability and grammatical flow.
Whether the generated note includes all clinically relevant information from the encounter. Critical safety dimension — omissions can affect care decisions.
Whether the generated note accurately reflects what was said, without fabricating or distorting clinical facts. Among the most safety-critical metrics.
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, SQ - System Quality

measures.description: Enhanced transcription metric assessing accurate capture of medical terminology specifically — goes beyond general WER for clinical relevance.
measures.dimensions: SQ - System Quality, IQ - Information Quality

measures.description: Degree of agreement between LLM automated scoring and human expert evaluation, used to validate the scalability of AI-based review approaches.
Specific sub-metric tracking whether newly prescribed or discussed medications are accurately documented in the note. Identified as a particular weakness.

id: Wojda 2025 - HCI of Ambient AI Qualitative Study
title: Understanding the Human-Computer Interaction of Ambient AI in Healthcare: A Qualitative Study of Early Provider Experiences
authors: Wojda, Thomas R.; Savio, Francis Vino Dominic; Hochheiser, Harry; Amaravathi, Anusha; Bharat, Krish; Fischer, Gary
journal: IntechOpen (open-access book chapter) — Published July 3, 2025
product: [[Abridge]] and [[Nuance DAX]] — both deployed across UPMC (University of Pittsburgh Medical Center). Products not compared directly; analysis focuses on combined early-adopter experience.
setting: Large integrated health system (UPMC). Mixed specialties. Survey-based qualitative study with 204 respondents one month post-implementation; 103 provided free-text feedback analyzed thematically.
evidenceLevel: Qualitative (thematic analysis embedded in a mixed-methods evaluation)
dataSources: Survey/self-report (open-ended survey responses; sentiment analysis + interrater reliability assessment)
summary: This study examined provider HCI experiences one month after ambient AI implementation across UPMC, using reflexive thematic analysis of open-ended survey responses. Six major themes emerged: (1) improved efficiency and workflow, (2) enhanced patient interaction, (3) documentation quality and editing burden, (4) learning curve and workflow adaptation, (5) specialty-specific performance variability, and (6) provider skepticism and AI trust. Sentiment analysis showed a balanced distribution of positive, negative, and mixed perceptions. The study highlights that while time savings and patient engagement improved, concerns around accuracy, editing load, and privacy persisted.
hypothesis: Not explicitly stated. Study is exploratory/inductive; aims to generate hypotheses for future study rather than test a specific hypothesis.

measures.description: Provider-reported perceptions of time savings and workflow improvement after ambient AI adoption, captured through open-ended survey responses.
measures.dimensions: US - User Satisfaction, II - Individual Impact

measures.description: Clinician-reported experience of enhanced presence and engagement with patients when freed from documentation tasks during encounters.
measures.dimensions: US - User Satisfaction, OI - Organizational Impact

measures.description: Provider perceptions of note accuracy and the additional effort required to review, correct, and edit AI-generated notes before sign-off.
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, US - User Satisfaction

measures.description: Qualitative capture of the adjustment period and training challenges providers experienced integrating ambient AI into existing workflows.
Provider-reported variation in ambient AI usefulness across clinical specialties, noting where the tool excelled or fell short.
measures.dimensions: SQ - System Quality, IQ - Information Quality

measures.description: Thematic coding of concerns around AI reliability, patient privacy, data use, and overall trust in the technology.
Automated sentiment analysis of free-text responses, classified as positive, negative, or mixed — used to characterize the aggregate emotional valence of early adoption.
DOI: 10.5772/intechopen.1011340

id: You 2025 - Ambient Documentation Technology Documentation Burden Burnout
title: Ambient Documentation Technology in Clinician Experience of Documentation Burden and Burnout
authors: You, Jacqueline G.; Dbouk, Reema H.; Landman, Adam; Ting, David Y.; Dutta, Sayon; Wang, Julie C.; Centi, Amanda J.; Macfarlane, Molly; Bechor, Eran; Letourneau, Jonathan; Choo-Kang, Gabrielle; Kim, Esther H.; Magee, Cordula; Lang, Brian J.; Angelo, Laura; Olin, Jackson; Frits, Michelle; Iannaccone, Christine; Rui, Angela; Salmikova, Ivana; Holland, Christopher; Blanchette, Bryan; Silverman, Rachel; Bates, David W.; Rotenstein, Lisa; Mishuris, Rebecca G.
journal: JAMA Network Open | 2025
product: Not specified by name. Ambient documentation technology deployed across Mass General Brigham (MGB, Boston) and Emory Healthcare (Atlanta). Mixed-methods evaluation of a real-world implementation.
setting: Two large academic health systems: Mass General Brigham (Boston) and Emory Healthcare (Atlanta). Multi-specialty outpatient and inpatient settings. Mixed-methods pre/post survey design.
evidenceLevel: Observational (pre/post mixed-methods: quantitative surveys + qualitative analysis)
dataSources: Survey/self-report | Mixed (qualitative + quantitative survey instruments)
summary: This mixed-methods study evaluated ambient documentation technology at MGB and Emory, using pre- and post-implementation surveys to assess clinician experience with documentation burden and burnout. The study collected both quantitative Likert-scale responses and qualitative free-text data across multiple specialty types. Survey domains included EHR burden, burnout indicators, well-being, and documentation workflow experience. The study provides one of the larger multi-site mixed-methods evaluations in the corpus and is notable for including both academic and community settings across two geographically distinct health systems.
hypothesis: Not explicitly stated. Implicit hypothesis: implementation of ambient documentation technology will reduce self-reported documentation burden and improve measures of burnout and clinician well-being.

measures.description: Clinician-reported experience of administrative load from EHR documentation tasks, measured via pre/post survey. Core outcome capturing subjective efficiency impact.
measures.dimensions: US - User Satisfaction, II - Individual Impact

measures.description: Self-reported burnout symptoms assessed via validated instruments in pre/post surveys. Primary well-being outcome of the evaluation.
Clinician-rated satisfaction with and perceived efficiency of EHR-related workflows, including time-on-task and friction points.
measures.dimensions: US - User Satisfaction, II - Individual Impact

measures.description: Qualitative and quantitative assessment of how clinicians experience the workflow of creating clinical notes — including ease, speed, and post-visit editing requirements.
measures.dimensions: US - User Satisfaction, II - Individual Impact

measures.description: Categorization of clinicians by degree of ambient tool adoption (stratified in analysis by usage level). Used to examine dose-response relationship between usage and outcomes.
Analysis of outcomes stratified by specialty category — surgical, primary care, medical subspecialty — to identify differential effects of ambient technology.
measures.dimensions: US - User Satisfaction, II - Individual Impact

measures.description: DOI: 10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2025.28056

id: van Buchem 2021 - Digital Scribe Scoping Review Research Agenda
title: The digital scribe in clinical practice: a scoping review and research agenda
authors: van Buchem, Marloes M.; Candel, Boris G.J.M.; Haan, Jeroen; Engelen, Laurens; Leeuw, Adriaan W.; van Gils, Mark; de Bruijne, Marleen C.; Moorman, P.W.
journal: npj Digital Medicine | Vol. 4 | Article 32 | 2021 (published online March 2021)
product: No commercial ambient AI products evaluated directly. 20 academic studies on digital scribe systems reviewed. Only DeepScribe provided unpublished performance data when contacted; Robin Healthcare, Nuance, Amazon, Saykara, Sopris Health did not respond. Babylonian Health (Google), Nuance mentioned as major commercial players.
setting: Scoping review — no single clinical setting. Studies spanned general (unspecified), primary care, home hemodialysis, orthopedic, cardiology, family medicine, internal medicine, telemedicine.
evidenceLevel: Scoping/Systematic Review
dataSources: Literature review (Medline, Web of Science, ACL, Arxiv); industry outreach
summary: This is a scoping review (PRISMA-ScR) of digital scribe technology through December 2020. Of 2,348 articles screened, 20 were included. 3 focused on ASR for clinical conversations; 17 used manual transcripts for NLP tasks (entity extraction, classification, summarization). Only 2 of 20 studies examined clinical validity/usability; none investigated clinical utility. Companies actively developing digital scribes do not publish performance data — only DeepScribe responded to outreach. The paper identifies a critical gap: the field is stuck at technical validity, far from clinical deployment readiness.
hypothesis: Not explicitly stated. Research questions: (1) What methods are used to develop digital scribes? (2) How accurate are these methods? (3) Have these been evaluated in clinical practice?

measures.description: Accuracy of automatic speech recognition on clinical conversations (word error rate)
Entity extraction, classification, summarization accuracy on clinical transcripts
Did any studies evaluate the tool with actual clinicians in clinical practice?
Did any studies assess patient or clinical outcomes in real-world use?
Whether commercial companies provided performance data when requested

keyFindings: 20 studies included from 2,348 screened. Only 2 assessed clinical validity/usability. Zero assessed clinical utility.
15/20 studies were performed by or in collaboration with a company — but companies still don't publish performance data.
Of 6 companies contacted (Robin Healthcare, DeepScribe, Saykara, Sopris Health, Amazon, Nuance), only DeepScribe provided data.
Most promising NLP approaches use context-sensitive word embeddings + attention-based neural networks (i.e., early transformer models).
Key conclusion: field is stuck at technical validity phase; clinical validity, usability, and clinical utility remain almost entirely unstudied as of 2020.
Research agenda: more transparent reporting, iterative study from technical → clinical validity, investigation of clinical utility.

id: van Buchem 2024 - Digital Scribe Documentation Time Quality Usability
title: Impact of a Digital Scribe System on Clinical Documentation Time and Quality: Usability Study
authors: van Buchem, Marieke Meija; Kant, Ilse M. J.; King, Liza; Kazmaier, Jacqueline; Steyerberg, Ewout W.; Bauer, Martijn P.
journal: JMIR AI | Vol. 3 | Published: September 23, 2024
product: **Autoscriber** (Autoscriber B.V., Eindhoven, Netherlands) — a commercially available Dutch-language digital scribe system using NLP for automated note generation from clinical consultations.
setting: Simulation-based usability study. Medical students with clinical experience (n=22) created 430 summaries of mock consultations. Three conditions: manual summarization, fully automated AI summaries, and automated summaries with manual editing. Leiden University Medical Center, Netherlands.
evidenceLevel: Simulation (controlled usability study using mock consultations with medical students; not real clinical deployment)
dataSources: Mixed (direct timing + validated quality instrument PDQI-9 + automated NLP metrics ROUGE/BERTScore + user survey)
summary: This Dutch usability study tested a digital scribe (Autoscriber) on 430 mock consultations with 22 medical students. Comparing manual, auto, and auto+edit conditions: automated summaries took slightly less time (186s vs 202s for editing, manual baseline) but scored worse on the PDQI-9 documentation quality instrument (median 25 vs 31). Auto summaries had higher word counts but lower lexical diversity. Student perceptions were mixed — ease of use and time-saving potential noted, but criticized for longer, more rigid structure. Study highlights that blanket automation may not improve quality without editing, and that benefit varies by individual.
hypothesis: Not explicitly stated. Implicit hypothesis: a digital scribe system will reduce documentation time without compromising quality.

measures.description: Time in seconds to complete clinical note summary across the three conditions (manual vs. auto vs. auto+edit). Direct timing measurement.
A validated 9-item instrument for rating clinical note quality across dimensions including accuracy, organization, and relevance. Blinded reassignment of notes for scoring.
Number of words in the generated summary. Auto-generated notes had significantly higher word counts — potential indicator of note bloat.
Measure of vocabulary variety in the note text. Lower in auto-generated notes, suggesting more formulaic/repetitive language.
Automated text overlap metric comparing AI-generated summaries to manual reference summaries.
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, SQ - System Quality

measures.description: Semantic similarity metric using contextual embeddings to compare generated vs. reference summaries.
measures.dimensions: IQ - Information Quality, SQ - System Quality

measures.description: Student-reported perceptions of the digital scribe's ease of use and utility, captured via post-study survey.
